source.title: Orchestrated control-plane swap and fallback playbook
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body:
Purpose and scope

This playbook defines a repeatable, low-risk process to orchestrate a control-plane swap between Redwood's hosted control plane and a customer-private control plane (or between two private control-plane versions) with explicit fallback and rollback steps. It covers: pre-swap readiness checks, phased swap orchestration, KV cache handover, traffic steering, verification gates, automated rollback triggers, and post-swap evidence collection. This document is intended for operators, field engineers, and SREs responsible for private deployments.

When to use this playbook

- Planned upgrades to control-plane software where a full in-place upgrade is impractical (major version or architecture change).
- Emergency diversion from hosted control plane to private control plane (or vice-versa) for customer isolation or regulatory requirements.
- Migration between control-plane clusters for scale, geo-failover, or tenancy rebalancing.

Key services referenced

- control-plane-api: the HTTP/GRPC endpoint handling tenant and model routing policy decisions.
- runtime-proxy: the data-plane proxy used at customer VPC edge to route inference traffic.
- operator-agent: the field operator service that performs image swap, config apply, and evidence bundle collection.
- kv-store (redis-ephemeral / s3-backed snapshot): stores runtime ephemeral state and prefix/KV caches.
- telemetry-gateway: aggregates traces, metrics, and log events for SLO validation.

SLOs and safety thresholds (gates)

- Successful RPC error-rate during canary window must remain < 0.5% (control-plane-api).
- 99th percentile control-plane latency must not increase by > 40% compared to pre-swap baseline.
- KV cache warmup hit-rate target >= 65% after model handover within 15 minutes.
- No more than 2% of tenants may exhibit critical inference latency regressions (>500ms increase) during the swap.

Pre-swap checklist (operator must verify)

1. Backups & evidence
   - Take a snapshot of control-plane database and model-variant manifest: operator-agent cli: run "operator-agent snapshot --target control-plane-db --tag pre-swap-<date>".
   - Export KV cache manifest and last-consistent cursor for each tenant: "kvctl export --out pre-swap-kv-manifest.json".
2. Capacity & compatibility
   - Confirm target control-plane nodes meet runtime compatibility matrix (see table below).
   - Reserve burst capacity of at least 20% in the serving cluster to avoid overload during cache miss storms.
3. Observability & test harness
   - Ensure telemetry-gateway retention window covers pre-swap baseline metrics.
   - Provision a synthetic workload (see Quick test harness) with representative sequence lengths and request mix.
4. Communication
   - Notify customer NOC and internal stakeholders; create incident runbook with oncall escalation list.

Compatibility matrix (example)

| Component | Minimum supported version | Recommended version | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| control-plane-api | v2.8.0 | v3.0.x | v3.0 adds policy caching improvements; requires runtime-proxy >= v1.4 |
| runtime-proxy | v1.3.2 | v1.5.x | v1.5 contains KV handoff endpoint and improved timeout handling |
| operator-agent | v0.9.0 | v1.1.x | operator-agent v1.x supports evidence bundle compression |
| kv-store | redis 6.2+ or s3 snapshot | redis 7.x | When using redis, enable AOF snapshots for quick restore |

Phased orchestration (high-level)

1) Canary phase (single-tenant/small slice)
   - Select canary tenant(s) using: high-traffic + tolerant=FALSE labels. Canary traffic should represent 1-3% of typical load.
   - Steps:
     a) Deploy control-plane software to new cluster (or enable secondary control plane) in standby mode.
     b) Configure routing policy rule: traffic for canary tenant -> new-control-plane endpoint. Use routing policy key: "swap-canary-<id>".
     c) Start synthetic workload and watch SLOs for 10 minutes.
     d) Validate KV cache warmup and inference tail latency.

2) Incremental rollout (progressive tenant fanout)
   - Expand to 10% of tenants over next 30 minutes if metrics remain green. Each expansion step: 10 -> 25 -> 50 -> 100% using predefined tenant cohorts.
   - At each step, validate: error-rate < 0.5%, p99 latency delta < 40%, KV warmup >= 65%.

3) Full swap and quarantine windows
   - Once at 100% and stable for 60 minutes, mark new control plane as primary and trigger a final state sync.
   - Hold a 2-hour quarantine window during which a coordinated rollback is still considered low-impact.

Automatic rollback triggers

If any of the following occur within the defined observation window, execute an immediate rollback to the previous control plane and follow the rollback evidence checklist:

- Total error-rate exceeds 1.5% for the canary group for > 3 minutes.
- p99 latency increases by > 100% for more than 5 minutes.
- KV warmup hit-rate below 40% after 15 minutes for more than 5 tenants.
- More than 5 tenants report inference failures measured by application-level 5xx rates.

Rollback sequence (fast path)

1. Freeze routing changes: set routing policy to failover -> previous-control-plane. Command: 
   - curl -X POST https://control-plane-admin.internal/apply-routing -H 'Authorization: Bearer <operator-token>' -d '{"mode":"failover","target":"previous-control-plane"}'
2. Scale down new control-plane traffic adapter to drain connections: "kubectl scale deployment cp-adapter-new --replicas=0 -n redwood-control"
3. Restore KV snapshot if necessary: "kvctl import --from pre-swap-kv-manifest.json --apply"
4. Rehydrate evidence bundle: operator-agent collect --tag pre-swap-<date> --include logs,metrics,traces
5. Notify stakeholders: runbook-trigger email templates located at /templates/rollback-notify.md

Post-rollback diagnostics

- Collect control-plane-api logs (last 2 hours) and grep for errors with code >= 500.
- Correlate telemetry-gateway traces to identify tail latency causation.
- Run model-sanity test suite against both control-plane versions to detect behavioral differences.

Operator CLI quick commands (examples)

- Start a canary swap:
  operator-agent start-swap --target new-control-plane --tenants canary-list.json --dry-run=false --evidence-tag pre-swap-<date>
- Monitor swap progress:
  operator-agent status --swap-id <id> --format table
- Force immediate rollback:
  operator-agent rollback --swap-id <id> --reason "metric-trigger" --collect-evidence

KV cache handover considerations

- Strategy A: live stream bulk prefetch
  - Pre-compute top-K prefixes per-tenant and seed target runtime-proxy via kvctl seed --manifest pre-swap-kv-manifest.json.
  - Advantages: faster warmup. Disadvantages: bandwidth and storage transient spike.

- Strategy B: on-demand lazy warming with throttled misses
  - Allow misses to fall back to model origin with a throttled concurrent limit to protect model backend.
  - Advantages: minimal up-front cost. Disadvantages: higher initial latency for some tenants.

Decision guidance: use Strategy A for latency-sensitive workloads with predictable prefix distributions (chatbots, search reranking). Use Strategy B when bandwidth or storage for full prefetch is constrained.

Observability checklist (what to watch)

- control-plane-api: RPC error-rate, p50/p95/p99 latencies, GC pause spikes.
- runtime-proxy: connection churn, per-tenant KV hit-rate, serialization errors.
- telemetry-gateway: missing spans, ingestion lag > 30s triggers alert.
- infra: CPU steal, node OOMs, disk IO saturation.

Synthetic test harness (quick)

1. Use the canonical synthetic client: redwood-test-client v1.2.
2. Load pattern: 60% short requests (<64 tokens), 30% medium (64-512 tokens), 10% long (>512 tokens).
3. Run in three phases: baseline (15m), canary (10m), expanded (continuous during rollout).

Evidence and audit bundle (required)

- Must include: control-plane-db snapshot id, kv manifest, operator-agent action log, telemetry slice (baseline and during swap), pod-level logs for cp-adapter and runtime-proxy, incident timeline.
- Bundle format: tar.gz with JSON manifest describing included files and checksums. Command to collect: "operator-agent collect-evidence --swap-id <id> --out /tmp/swap-evidence-<id>.tar.gz"

Troubleshooting playbook (short)

- Symptom: KV warmup low despite prefetch
  - Check redis AOF/memory pressure; ensure eviction policy is not aggressive. Run: "redis-cli INFO MEMORY" and "kvctl show-evictions --tenant <id>".
- Symptom: sudden p99 tail spike
  - Check for long GC pauses in control-plane API pods (describe pod, jstack if possible), and review recent kernel updates or node-level maintenance.
- Symptom: gradual increase in error-rate after 30m
  - Inspect model-variant manifest divergence and verify model artifact checksums across clusters (sha256).

Roll-forward (if canary passed and gradual rollout completed)

- Finalize primary assignment in the global routing table and mark old control plane as read-only for 24 hours.
- Schedule decommission window for the old control plane only after 7 days of stable telemetry and no rollback incidents.

Ownership and contacts

- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments (oncall: private-deployments-sre@redwood.internal).
- Secondary owner: eng-serving-runtime (runtime-lead@redwood.internal).
- Escalation: Platform Pager (pager redwood-platform) -> Site Reliability Lead.

Related artifacts and references

- Upgrade decision record: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/upgrade-and-rollback/incremental-stateful-upgrade-strategy-2027
- KV migration playbook: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/kv-cache-migrations-and-runtime-state-migration-playbook-2025
- Operator CLI reference: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/private-deploy-operator-cheat-sheet-for-field-installations-2027

Revision history

- 2027-11-02: Drafted by Maya Chen.
- 2028-03-12: Reviewed and published; added automated rollback triggers and more detailed KV handover guidance.

source.title: Controlled rollout and compatibility gates: private deployment guide
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview
========

Purpose: This guide defines a single, repeatable process for performing controlled rollouts of Redwood Private deployments (VPC and on-prem) that enforces compatibility gates across runtime, model artifacts, packaging, and network/KMS dependencies. It collates pre-installation checks, staged rollout steps, day-2 validation, rollback criteria, and customer-facing readiness items so that field installs, upgrades, and hot-swap model rollouts are low-risk and observable.

Scope: applies to: 
- New environment installs (VPC private clusters and on-prem air-gapped installs)
- Versioned upgrades of the control plane and serving runtime
- Model binary swaps that change kernel/quantization profiles or KV cache format

Owners and stakeholders
-----------------------
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments (Deployment Engineering)
- Runbook owner: on-call Private Deploys SRE rotation
- Security & signing owner: security-and-compliance (image signing and KMS policy)
- Product owner: product (Private deployments roadmap)

Key principles
--------------
- Block unsafe changes early: automated gates should prevent incompatible artifacts from reaching customer infra.
- Incremental blast radius: staged canaries, region-by-region or tenant-by-tenant rollouts.
- Observable safety nets: meaningful telemetry, alerting, and automated rollback when SLO regressions are detected.
- Reproducible preflight: deterministic prechecks customers or field engineers can run prior to any change.

Preflight checklist (run before any install/upgrade)
--------------------------------------------------
1) Customer / environment readiness (owner: field engineer)
   - Hardware: nodes meet minimums in the Customer Requirements table below.
   - Network: egress to Redwood control plane (if hybrid), internal peering, and MTU >= 1500.
   - KMS: key accessible from the control plane subnet; test encrypt/decrypt within maintenance window.
   - Telemetry endpoints: prometheus/OTLP endpoints reachable (or local relay configured).
2) Artifact and package validation (owner: deploy engineering)
   - Image signature verification: image digest matches signatures in signed release manifest.
   - Model artifact compatibility: model .bin and quant profiles validated against the runtime compatibility matrix.
   - Kernel and libc compatibility: verify kernel ABI list in the release notes matches target hosts.
3) Backups and rollback window
   - KV cache snapshots exported (if performing stateful upgrades).
   - Control plane backup stored in customer-approved bucket (retention >= 72 hours).
4) SLO and baseline capture
   - Capture baseline latency percentile (p50/p95/p99), token/sec throughput and error rate for the active model/prefix distribution.

Minimum customer requirements (short table)
------------------------------------------
| Requirement | Minimum | Notes |
|-------------|---------|-------|
| GPU | 1 x A10 or equivalent per 4 vCPU | For small trial clusters; production requires validated GPU pool sizing |
| RAM per host | 64 GB | Host RAM for batching and KV cache |
| Disk | 500 GB NVMe | OS + model shard storage; larger for large models |
| Network | 10 Gbps | Prefer 25 Gbps for high-throughput tenants |
| KMS | AES-256 key with CMK policy | Must allow decrypt from control plane subnet |

Compatibility gates (automated, enforced)
-----------------------------------------
- Signature gate: Docker/OCI images and model bundles must be signed by a trusted Redwood release key.
- Schema gate: model metadata.json must include runtime_version, quant_profile, and kv_format_version fields that match allowed values.
- Kernel gate: upgrade job will compare host kernel ABI list to release-supported kernels and abort if mismatch.
- Config gate: active config flags (batching_enabled, prefix_cache_size_mb) must be present and within allowed ranges.

Staged rollout pattern (recommended)
----------------------------------
1) Canary (internal): deploy to internal staging cluster with production-weight traffic replay for 12h. Verify: no increase in p95 latency > +10% and error rate < +0.1% absolute.
2) Customer shadow (opt-in): select 1–2 low-impact customers for shadowing model or runtime for 24–48h.
3) Gradual tenant ramp: 10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100% of customer’s tenant capacity over >48h with automated checks at each step.
4) Global rollout: region-by-region with hold windows and capacity gates.

Automated verification checks (run after each stage)
---------------------------------------------------
- Latency regression: compare live p50/p95/p99 to baseline; regression thresholds: p95 > +20% or p99 > +25% triggers immediate pause.
- Error budget burn: if error rate consumes >30% of error budget in a 1h window, pause and rollback.
- Token throughput drop: sustained throughput drop >15% relative to baseline for 30m triggers investigation.
- Model quality check (if applicable): run representative prompt set against canary model; if quality score drops by >3 points, hold.

Deployment commands and samples
------------------------------
- Verify image signature: 
```bash
redwoodctl images verify --manifest signed-release-v2.json --keyring /etc/redwood/keys/release.pub
```
- Run preflight checks (example CLI):
```bash
redwoodctl preflight --env staging --checks network,kms,signature,kernel
```
- Example deployment fragment (YAML):
```yaml
apiVersion: redwood/v1
kind: PrivateDeploy
metadata:
  name: private-deploy-canary
spec:
  controlPlaneVersion: 2027-02-03
  servingRuntimeVersion: v2.8.1
  canaryPercent: 10
  modelBundle: s3://releases/models/chat-q4f/signed-manifest.json
  gates:
    - signature
    - schema
    - kernel
```

Day-2 validation playbook (30–120 minutes after stage)
-----------------------------------------------------
1) Confirm service endpoints: all pods reported Ready and readiness probes passing.
2) Query health endpoints: /health/runtime, /health/kvs, /health/eviction returning 200.
3) Validate telemetry: verify metrics present for token emit, request_count, tail_latency distribution.
4) Run sample prompts against canary tenants and compare structured outputs against golden responses.
5) Verify backups: KV snapshot audit appears in the backup log within the window.

Rollback decision matrix
------------------------
- Immediate rollback triggers (automatic):
  - p99 latency > +30% sustained for 10m
  - error rate spike to >2% absolute for core endpoints for 5m
  - model format mismatch detected (runtime cannot load model)
  - image signature validation failure on any host
- Manual rollback triggers (requires triage):
  - subtle model quality regressions detected in human-in-loop QA
  - intermittent OOMs correlated with config changes

Rollback procedure (stateful and stateless)
------------------------------------------
1) Stateless (serving runtime only):
   a) Scale down new version: redwoodctl deploy promote --to previous --grace-period 120
   b) Monitor p95/p99 for 15 minutes for stabilization
2) Stateful (KV cache or metadata format changes):
   a) Trigger controlled KV restore from snapshot: redwoodctl kv restore --snapshot-id <id> --target <cluster>
   b) Drain write traffic using routing policy and keep read-only for degraded capacity
   c) Reintroduce previous runtime binary with pinned model bundle digest

Packaging and signing notes (on-prem specifics)
---------------------------------------------
- Air-gapped packaging: release tarballs are produced with deterministic hashes and bundled with a release-signature.asc file. Field installers must validate signatures with the offline keyring shipped on the installer USB.
- Container image verification: even when using a private registry, the release manifest (signed) must be mirrored and verified against its digest.
- Firmware/kernel prechecks: the installer will run kernel-compat checks and fail-fast if ABI list is incompatible; provide a "--force" flag only for controlled experiments and require security approval.

Customer-facing readiness items (what to provide to the customer)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Preflight report: PDF with network, KMS, and telemetry verification results.
- Upgrade window and rollback SLAs: agreed maintenance window, expected downtime (if any), and RTO targets.
- Contact list: field engineer, SRE lead, and security contact with 24/7 escalation phone numbers.

Telemetry and alerting configuration (recommended thresholds)
------------------------------------------------------------
- Alerts to create in customer/central monitoring: 
  - redwood.runtime.latency.p95 > baseline*1.2 for 15m (sev-2)
  - redwood.runtime.error_rate > baseline+0.01 for 5m (sev-2)
  - redwood.kv.restore.failure > 0 (sev-1)
  - image.signature.invalid detected on host (sev-1)

Long-form examples and appendices
--------------------------------
- Appendix A: Example release manifest (JSON snippet)
  {
    "release": "2027-02-03",
    "images": ["sha256:..."],
    "signed_by": "rel-team@redwood.internal"
  }

- Appendix B: Preflight CLI full output example (truncated)
  - NETWORK OK: egress ports 443, 9411 reachable
  - KMS OK: sample encrypt/decrypt succeeded
  - SIGNATURE OK: image digest matches signature in signed-release-v2.json

- Appendix C: Escalation matrix (names and pager IDs) - kept in team wiki for PII reasons.

Related references
------------------
- See: container-image-signing-and-verification-playbook (onprem-packaging)
- See: private-deploy-onboarding-and-failover-runbook (deployment-guides)
- See: kv-cache-migrations-and-runtime-state-migration-playbook (day-2-ops)

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-20: created by Ava Chen (initial draft)
- 2026-12-15: added controlled rollout thresholds and packaging notes (review by Diego Martinez)
- 2027-02-10: clarified rollback matrix and preflight automation (approved)

source.title: Private deploy networking bridge and fallback patterns
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

This document describes recommended networking bridge and fallback patterns for Redwood Private (VPC and on‑prem) deployments. It focuses on field-safe connectivity designs that preserve control‑plane reachability, allow safe model hot‑swaps, and provide deterministic failure modes when network paths are constrained (air‑gapped customers, intermittent interconnects, or restricted egress environments).

Audience

- Deployment engineers executing customer installs
- Field operations and SRE teams responsible for day‑2 networking
- Solutions engineering and customer success for pre‑sales readiness checks

Scope and goals

- Provide a small set of vetted bridge and fallback patterns that balance security, observability, and reliability.
- Document rollout and rollback procedures for network modifications without requiring full service restarts.
- List concrete customer requirements and acceptance criteria for connectivity.
- Include example configs, iptables/systemd snippets, and monitoring queries.

Patterns summary (high level)

1) Local bridge + control-plane proxy (recommended for constrained egress)
- A lightweight sidecar proxy (redwood-bridge-proxy) runs in customer VPC/on‑prem to relay control plane and telemetry over a single allowed outbound connection.
- Strongly preferred when customers allow only outbound TLS over port 443.
- Supports transparent retry and multiplexing of control messages.

2) Mutual TLS (mTLS) site-to-site tunnel with ephemeral keys
- Strongly recommended for customers who allow inbound VPN-like tunnels from a fixed IP range.
- Use ephemeral certificates rotated by the operator every 24–72 hours.
- Falls back to pattern 1 when site-to-site connectivity flaps.

3) Queued store-and-forward bridge for air-gapped segments
- Useful for batch workloads where strict real-time control is optional.
- Control plane messages are queued to an operator-managed handoff node; large model artifacts are staged via signed bundles on removable media or a dedicated transfer appliance.

4) Sidecar proxy with local KV-cache mirroring for model hot-swap safety
- Maintains a local LRU prefix/KV cache copy to support short-term inference traffic during transient control-plane outages.
- Cache TTLs and size are operator-configurable and critical to avoid stale state.

Architecture (text diagram)

Customer VPC / On‑Prem Network
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+
  | Tenant nodes (inference)  -- redwood-sidecar -- redwood-bridge-proxy |
  |    |
  |    +--> Local KV cache (redis-lite)
  |
  |  (WAN / Interconnect) <--- mTLS tunnel or TLS egress to Redwood control plane
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+

SLOs, thresholds and failure modes

Recommended SLOs
- Control plane heartbeat: 99.9% of 10s heartbeats succeed per 30d window.
- Model management commands (deploy/undeploy): median completion < 45s when control plane reachable.
- Telemetry upload: 95% of agent metrics delivered within 5 minutes.

Key thresholds and operator actions
- If heartbeat drops below 99.0% for 10 minutes: open P0 to field ops and trigger local cache scale-up.
- If control plane unavailable for > 30 minutes and queued job backlog exceeds 5k requests: enable degraded mode (reject new long-running requests and surface a 503 to upstream).

Pre‑installation customer requirements matrix

| Requirement | Minimum | Preferred | Why it matters |
|------------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Allowlist IPs | outbound to 52.14.32.0/24 | outbound to dedicated operator pool | control plane connectivity |
| Ports | 443 outbound TLS | 443 outbound, 7843 for mTLS | single-port simplicity vs tunnel performance |
| MTU | 1400 | 9000 supported | large model artifact transfer reliability |
| Internal DNS | resolvable hostnames for control-plane endpoints | private DNS forwarding | certificate SAN and SNI consistency |
| KMS integration | local keywrap supported | HSM/KMS with rotation | customer encryption-at-rest requirements |
| Latency to operator endpoints | < 150ms | < 50ms | model deploy and healthchecks responsiveness |
| Egress policy | allow outbound to operator endpoints | allow mutual tunnels | feature enablement (telemetry, live rollouts) |

Configuration table (examples)

| Component | Example setting | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| redwood-bridge-proxy | --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --cert-rotate=72h | single process for egress with auto rotation |
| sidecar-cache | max-entries=100000 ttl=2m | protect memory while providing prefix cache for transient disconnects |
| healthcheck | /healthz interval=10s timeout=3s | quick failure detection and automatic local fallback |

Note: above table rendered here as monospace YAML example below.

Example redwood-bridge-proxy systemd unit (recommended)

[Unit]
Description=Redwood Bridge Proxy
After=network-online.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/redwood-bridge-proxy --egress-host=control.redwood.ai:443 --log-level=info --cert-rotate=72h
Restart=on-failure
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

iptables minimal outbound allowlist example

# allow outbound HTTPS to operator pool
iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d 52.14.32.0/24 --dport 443 -m state --state NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
# allow local loopback
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT
# drop everything else by default (customer may use more sophisticated rules)
iptables -A OUTPUT -j DROP

Model artifact transfer checklist (airgap-friendly)

- Ensure artifact bundles are signed with operator key and timestamped.
- Validate SHA256 checksum on receiving appliance.
- Verify compat matrix: model version -> runtime kernel build (see reference-architectures page).
- If MTU < 1400, enable chunked transfer and resume support.

Deployment steps: safe networking change (step-by-step)

1) Preflight (field engineer)
  a) Run connectivity-check.sh against operator endpoints (script below).
  b) Confirm DNS resolution and TLS validation with openssl s_client.
  c) Validate firewall rules with tcpdump and simulated egress.
2) Enable bridge proxy in monitor-only mode
  a) Deploy sidecar with --dry-run to observe connection attempts.
  b) Confirm proxy establishes TLS to control plane but does not accept control commands.
3) Switch control plane routing to bridge (gradual)
  a) Promote bridge proxy to active for 10% of traffic via traffic selector.
  b) Monitor heartbeats and control command latency for 15 minutes.
4) Full cutover
  a) Promote bridge for 100% traffic.
  b) Keep original path for 30 minutes as cold fallback.
5) Post validation
  a) Run synthetic inference tests (100 small requests, record latency and error rate).
  b) Verify telemetry ingestion and disk watermark for local cache.

Rollback procedure (networking changes)

1) If errors exceed thresholds (>=5% failure rate or control commands > 3x median latency) for 10 minutes, perform immediate rollback:
  a) Revert routing at traffic selector to previous path.
  b) If routing cannot be reverted, enable degraded mode and surface clear customer-facing error.
2) Postmortem steps
  a) Collect tcpdump and proxy logs from affected nodes.
  b) Escalate to networking SME and open internal incident.

Upgrade procedure (bridge proxy)

- Rolling upgrade (preferred):
  1) Mark one rack/node group for upgrade.
  2) Deploy new proxy image with canary config (monitor-only).
  3) Promote to active for 5% traffic for 10m.
  4) If metrics OK, proceed in waves (25%, 50%, 100%) with 15m observation windows.
- If proxy upgrade requires config change that is not backward compatible, perform co‑existence test and schedule maintenance window.

Day‑2 operations: monitoring, alerts, and runbook links

Essential metrics to export
- bridge_proxy.connection_success_rate (1m/5m/30m)
- bridge_proxy.cert_age_seconds
- sidecar_cache.hit_rate and eviction_rate
- controlplane.command_latency_p50/p95/p99
- telemetry.uplink_backlog_count

Suggested alerts (examples)
- P0: bridge_proxy.connection_success_rate < 95% for 5m -> page oncall SRE and field ops
- P1: controlplane.command_latency_p95 > 120s for 15m -> notify platform eng
- P2: sidecar_cache.eviction_rate > 0.5 for 10m -> capacity increase recommended

Runbook excerpts (quick actions)
- Recover proxy TLS: restart redwood-bridge-proxy service, check /var/log/redwood/bridge.log, rotate cert if expired.
- Rehydrate local cache: scale sidecar-cache up by 2x for 30 minutes and monitor eviction_rate.

Instrumentation examples (PromQL)
- bridge connection success rate (1m): sum(rate(bridge_proxy_success_total[1m])) / sum(rate(bridge_proxy_attempt_total[1m]))
- control command p95 latency: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(control_command_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))

Security and compliance notes

- All operator certificates must be logged and auditable; TF plan and inventory should be stored in customer-provided S3 or equivalent with ACLs.
- On air-gapped installs, use signed artifact manifests and enforce signature verification during consumption.
- KMS-integrated deployments must demonstrate key-rotation test prior to GA signoff.

Ownership and escalation

- Deployment owner: eng-private-deployments field team (Primary: Marco Alvarez).
- Networking SME: Diego Ramos.
- Oncall SRE: see /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations for current duty.
- Customer success escalation: contact mapped CSM for contract-specific SLAs before any service-affecting change.

Appendices

A) connectivity-check.sh (example)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
ENDPOINT=control.redwood.ai:443
echo "Resolving $ENDPOINT"
host=${ENDPOINT%:*}
port=${ENDPOINT#*:}
if ! getent hosts $host >/dev/null; then
  echo "DNS failure for $host"; exit 2
fi
openssl s_client -connect ${host}:${port} -servername ${host} -brief < /dev/null 2>&1 | sed -n '1,20p'
exit 0

B) Example minimal sidecar config (YAML)
component: sidecar-cache
settings:
  max_entries: 100000
  ttl_seconds: 120
  disk_persist: true
  persist_path: /var/lib/redwood/cache

References and related pages
- Edge gateway and airgapped bridge architecture (reference page)
- Private deploy onboarding and failover runbook
- Onprem packaging: container signing and verification playbook

Document maintenance
- Owners should review this page quarterly or whenever operator egress policy changes.
- Major updates must be coordinated with security and customer success (confidentiality: restricted).

title: Private deploy networking bridge and fallback patterns
preview:
Overview

This document describes recommended networking bridge and fallback patterns for Redwood Private (VPC and on‑prem) deployments. It focuses on field-safe connectivity designs that preserve control‑plane reachability, allow safe model hot‑swaps, and provide deterministic failure modes when network paths are constrained (air‑gapped customers, intermittent interconnects, or restricted egress environments).

Audience

- Deployment engineers executing customer installs
- Field operations and SRE teams responsible for day‑2 networking
- Solutions engineering and customer success for pre‑sales readiness checks

Scope and goals

- Provide a small set of vetted bridge and fallback patterns that balance security, observability, and reliability.
- Document rollout and rollback procedures for network modifications without requiring full service restarts.
- List concrete customer requirements and acceptance criteria for connectivity.
- Include example configs, iptables/systemd snippets, and monitoring queries.

Patterns summary (high level)

1) Local bridge + control-plane proxy (recommended for constrained egress)
- A lightweight sidecar proxy (redwood-bridge-proxy) runs in customer VPC/on‑prem to relay control plane and telemetry over a single allowed outbound connection.
- Strongly preferred when customers allow only outbound TLS over port 443.
- Supports transparent retry and multiplexing of control messages.

2) Mutual TLS (mTLS) site-to-site tunnel with ephemeral keys
- Strongly recommended for customers who allow inbound VPN-like tunnels from a fixed IP range.
- Use ephemeral certificates rotated by the operator every 24–72 hours.
- Falls back to pattern 1 when site-to-site connectivity flaps.

3) Queued store-and-forward bridge for air-gapped segments
- Useful for batch workloads where strict real-time control is optional.
- Control plane messages are queued to an operator-managed handoff node; large model artifacts are staged via signed bundles on removable media or a dedicated transfer appliance.

4) Sidecar proxy with local KV-cache mirroring for model hot-swap safety
- Maintains a local LRU prefix/KV cache copy to support short-term inference traffic during transient control-plane outages.
- Cache TTLs and size are operator-configurable and critical to avoid stale state.

Architecture (text diagram)

Customer VPC / On‑Prem Network
  +-----------------------------------------------------------+
  | Tenant nodes (inference)  -- redwood-sidecar -- redwood-bridge-proxy |
  |    |
  |    +--> Local KV cache (redis-lite)
  |
  |  (WAN / Interconnect) <--- mTLS tunnel or TLS egress to Redwood control plane

source.title: Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

High-level topology

1) Edge nodes (customer-managed in VPC or on-prem):
   - Small GPU or CPU instances running quantized model variants (4–8GB GPU or multi-socket CPU).
   - Serve frequent short requests locally with optimized kernels and minimal batching.
2) Regional aggregator (optional):
   - Responsible for request classification, caching, and short‑circuiting locality decisions.
3) Central Redwood Dedicated pool (hosted or customer collocated):
   - Large-GPU cluster (A100/H100 equivalents) for long-context, high‑quality responses.
4) Control plane connectors:
   - Secure control channel for model metadata, KV cache presence, and rollout policies.

Component details

- Local runtime: a stripped-down Redwood agent providing:
  - quantized-model runtime (int8/4-bit profiles),
  - prefix/KV cache with LRU TTL (default 24h),
  - small-batch path with kernel selection favoring latency.
- Classifier router: lightweight policy process that decides: LOCAL | CACHE_HIT | CENTRAL. Policies include tokens count, prompt length, customer labels, and per-route cost ceilings.
- Fallback policy: automatic reroute to central on local failure, plus degraded-mode reply when central unreachable.
- Observability: token-level traces, request sampling (1%) to central for quality checks, and latency histograms per-tier.

SLOs and thresholds (recommended starting values)

- Local path: p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms for requests with input tokens <= 64.
- Classification decision: < 5ms 99.9% of the time.
- Central path for long requests: p95 < 1.2s (depends on model).
- Cache hit target: >= 30% of local short requests.
- Availability: local agent SLA (operator target) 99.95% during business hours.

Customer requirements (pre-install checklist)

- Network:
  - LAN RTT to local nodes: < 2ms typical.
  - WAN RTT from edge to regional aggregator/central: < 100ms for acceptable fallbacks.
  - Ports: outbound TCP 443 to Redwood control plane and dedicated pool endpoints; open internal gRPC/TCP ports 8000–8020 for inter-agent.
- Hardware:
  - Edge GPU: minimum 4GB VRAM (recommended 8GB) for quantized variants; or 32 vCPUs with AVX512 for CPU-only sites.
  - Disk: 50GB NVMe for model shards, cache, and logs.
- Security & keys:
  - Customer KMS reachable in VPC for secret unwrap; HSM integration optional for FIPS customers.
  - Mutually-authenticated TLS between agents and central (mTLS).
- IAM & service accounts:
  - Dedicated service account with least privilege; key rotation schedule defined.

Deployment playbook (edge node)

1) Preflight (operator):
   - Validate hardware profile: run collect_hardware_profile.sh and store output.
   - Verify network rules and KMS access.
   - Ensure time sync (NTP) and kernel parameters per infra-standards.
2) Install agent:
   - curl -sSL https://packages.redwood.ai/agent/install.sh | sudo bash -s -- --profile=latency-edge
   - Configure /etc/redwood/agent.yaml with:
     - model_variant: "rw-small-quant-v1"
     - kv_cache.ttl: 24h
     - router.policy: "token-count-based"
3) Smoke test:
   - redwoodctl healthcheck --target localhost:8000
   - Run quick latency benchmark: redwoodctl bench --requests 500 --max_tokens 64
4) Canary rollout:
   - Update router policy to include 10% forced central routing to validate end-to-end. Monitor errors for 2 hours before full promotion.

Day-2 operations (operator runbook)

- Monitoring and alerting:
  - Metrics to watch: local_latency_ms (p50/p95), classifier_decision_latency_ms, kv_cache_hit_rate, model_load_errors, agent_restarts.
  - Alerts: kv_cache_hit_rate < 15% sustained 30m; agent_restarts > 3 in 10m; local p95 > 200ms for 15m.
- Cache warmup strategy:
  - Scheduled synthetic workloads during quiet hours to prefill KV caches for top 200 prompt templates.
- Capacity scaling:
  - When local CPU/GPU utilization > 70% for 30m, auto-scale policy triggers adding a hot-spare agent.
- Quality checks:
  - Sampled 1% of local replies are sent to central for quality comparison; alert if semantic drift > threshold.

Upgrade and rollback procedures

- Upgrade (agent or local model):
  1. Stage new model variant in a canary edge node (1 node). Run bench and quality regression checks against a saved prompt set.
  2. Gradually increase canary percentage (1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100%) over multiple hours while monitoring p95 latency and quality diffs.
  3. If quality regression or latency spike occurs, trigger rollback.

- Rollback (fast-fail path):
  - Command: redwoodctl agent rollback --target <agent-id> --to-version <previous-model-semilabel>
  - Automated safety: controller evaluates health for 10m before rolling to additional nodes; if failure persists, enact tenant traffic drain to central with retained cache headers.
  - Evidence capture: collect /var/log/redwood/agent.log, heap profiles, last 100 classifier traces. Attach to incident ticket.

Failure modes and mitigation

- Local model OOM: automatic restart into degraded/no-local mode (route to central) and notify operator.
- KV cache corruption: fallback to central until cache TTL expires and warmup job restores entries.
- Network partition (edge->central): operate in isolated mode, local-only responses; flag degraded quality for audit.

Observability and telemetry contract

- Required metrics exported via Prometheus endpoint:
  - redwood_local_request_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_classifier_decision_latency_ms_bucket
  - redwood_kv_cache_hit_ratio
  - redwood_model_load_duration_seconds
- Traces: OpenTelemetry spans must include decision.reason, route.tier, model.variant, and cache.hit boolean.
- Logs: structured JSON lines with request_id, tenant_id, model_variant, duration_ms, and error_codes. Retention default 30d (configurable).

Security & compliance notes

- All model artifacts signed and verified using the customer-side image attestation. See image signing playbook for commands.
- Sensitive telemetry (user prompts) must be redacted before external transmission; send only hashed prompt fingerprints when required.
- Key rotation: follow a 90-day rotation schedule and ensure agents gracefully reload keys without restart.

Ownership and contact points

- Architecture owner: Priya Natarajan (eng-serving-runtime).
- Support handoff: Private Deployments Field Team (private-deploy-support@redwood.ai).
- Oncall escalation: see eng-sre oncall roster; page: /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotation.

Appendix: example router policy (YAML)

---
policy_name: latency_fanout_v1
rules:
  - name: local_short_request
    condition: "input_tokens <= 64 and tenant_priority == 'standard'"
    action: LOCAL
    sampling: 0.01   # 1% sampled to central for QA
  - name: cache_only
    condition: "cache.hit == true"
    action: LOCAL
  - name: heavy_request
    condition: "input_tokens > 64 or requires_retrieval == true"
    action: CENTRAL
  - name: default_fallback
    action: CENTRAL

References and related reading

- Private deploy observability contract: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/observability-contracts-and-log-sampling-strategy-2026
- Image attestation and signing: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/image-attestation-spec-2026
- Cold start mitigation: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/cold-start-latency-mitigation-for-private-deploys-2026

Change log

- 2026-03-15: Initial draft.
- 2026-06-10: Added SLO targets and cache warmup strategy after field trials.
- 2026-10-12: Finalized rollback commands and telemetry contract.

title: Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture

Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

High-level topology

1) Edge nodes (customer-managed in VPC or on-prem):
   - Small GPU or CPU instances running quantized model variants (4–8GB GPU or multi-socket CPU).
   - Serve frequent short requests locally with optimized kernels and minimal batching.
2) Regional aggregator (optional):
   - Responsible for request classification, caching, and short‑circuiting locality decisions.
3) Central Redwood Dedicated pool (hosted or customer collocated):
   - Large-GPU cluster (A100/H100 equivalents) for long-context, high‑quality responses.
4) Control plane connectors:
   - Secure control channel for model metadata, KV cache presence, and rollout policies.

source.title: Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge Reference Architecture
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audits, and model-drift investigations. The design balances minimal performance impact, strong chain-of-custody guarantees, and customer privacy controls.

Goals:
- Provide an immutable, auditable snapshot pipeline for tenant state and request traces.
- Ensure snapshots can be collected with <=5% added tail-latency under normal load via asynchronous capture.
- Maintain tenant data isolation: all forensic snapshots are encrypted with customer KMS/HSM keys where required.
- Offer deterministic retention and evidence packaging for audits (customer-facing artifact).

When to use this architecture:
- Post-incident root cause analysis for private deployments where customer operators require local forensics.
- Regulatory evidence requests (e.g., finserv, healthcare) where full request-correspondence is needed.
- Model quality regression cases where sequence-level KV-caches and prefix traces are necessary to reproduce outputs.

High-level components:
1) Local Capture Agents (agent-snap): per-host process that performs non-blocking request tap, kv-cache snapshot coordination, and local staging.
2) Snapshot Bridge (bridge-proxy): tenant-scoped gateway that aggregates artifacts, signs bundles, and performs envelope encryption for transfer.
3) Forensic Store (immutable-store): append-only object store (S3-compatible) with versioned buckets and retention rules; may be hosted on customer object storage or Redwood-managed vault.
4) Evidence Broker (evidence-api): control-plane service to register retention policies, initiate snapshots, and produce audit manifests.
5) Operator Console (private console extension): UI + APIs for authorized operators to request, view, and package evidence bundles.

Architecture diagram (text):
- Tenant traffic -> Agent-snap (in host network namespace) -> local staging area -> Bridge-proxy (tenant egress to object store) -> Immutable-store (encrypted)
- Evidence Broker coordinates via control-plane channel (signed requests, attestation tokens)

Key design decisions and rationale:
- Asynchronous capture: Agent-snap never blocks request processing. Synchronous forcing would violate latency SLOs.
- Envelope encryption with customer KMS: ensures Redwood cannot read snapshots when customer requires BYOK.
- Minimal sensitive PII in bundled artifacts: by default we capture request-id, tokenized input/response, kv-cache state (configurable). Full plaintext capture requires explicit customer opt-in and attestation.

Detailed flow (step-by-step):
1) Trigger: Evidence request can be operator-initiated (console) or automated (alert rule). Evidence Broker validates the request, checks operator RBAC, and issues a short-lived signed attestation token to bridge-proxy.
2) Local Coordination: Agent-snap receives attestation token and enters "capture window" for the tenant (configurable 30s–10m). During the window it records non-blocking traces and initiates a consistent KV-cache snapshot across replicas using the runtime snapshot API.
3) Bundle creation: Agent-snap composes a manifest.json (metadata), a compressed trace file (.ndjson.gz), and a serialized kv-cache snapshot (.snap.gz). Each file is hashed (SHA-256) and recorded.
4) Signing & Encryption: Bridge-proxy validates attestation, signs the manifest with a bridge key, then performs envelope-encryption: file encrypted with data key; data key encrypted with customer KMS public key or Redwood-managed key depending on policy.
5) Upload & Index: Encrypted bundle uploaded to Immutable-store and indexed in Evidence Broker with metadata: tenant-id, originating host, time-range, SLOs at capture time, operator id. Evidence Broker generates an audit entry and exportable chain-of-custody record.
6) Retrieval: Authorized operators request retrieval. Evidence Broker verifies RBAC, returns decryption instructions (if Redwood-managed) or redirects to pre-signed customer storage download links (if BYOK), and records the retrieval event.

Operational SLOs and thresholds:
- Capture startup latency: agent-snap start < 200ms (metric: agent.capture.startup_ms P50/P95).
- Impact on tail latency: added P99 latency <= 5% of baseline for requests during capture windows.
- Upload success rate: 99.9% of bundles must reach Immutable-store within 2 minutes under normal network conditions.
- Chain-of-custody integrity: manifest and bundle signatures validated at time of retrieval or audit.

Alerting and monitoring (suggested):
- Alerts: agent.capture.failure_rate > 0.5% for 5m -> PagerDuty (oncall-owner: runtime-team)
- Alerts: bundle.upload.latency_p95 > 120s for 10m -> SRE team
- Dashboards: per-tenant capture activity, bundle sizes distribution, retention occupancy by customer, failed-encryption rates.

Operator runbooks (concise):
Runbook A — Investigate missing bundle:
1) Check Evidence Broker UI for "bundle failed" events. Note bundle_id.
2) SSH to originating host (use operator jumpbox): sudo journalctl -u agent-snap | grep bundle_id.
3) Validate local manifest and hash: sha256sum manifest.json -> compare with Evidence Broker index.
4) If encryption failed: check KMS connectivity (kms-proxy logs) and re-run bundle re-encrypt command: 
   bridge-proxy reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --retries 3
5) After fix, mark bundle re-uploaded in Evidence Broker and verify presence in Immutable-store.

Runbook B — Produce customer audit package:
1) Operator opens Evidence Broker -> select tenant -> time-range -> generate audit package.
2) Evidence Broker returns package_id and a pre-signed download link or decryption instructions.
3) For BYOK: instruct customer to perform local key unwrap and verify manifest signature.
4) Record the audit package delivery in customer QBR artifact store and notify the customer success lead.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (safe-to-execute checklist):
- Scope: agent-snap and bridge-proxy are the only services needing coordinated upgrade. Evidence Broker schema changes are backward-compatible.
- Preflight checks before upgrade:
  - Run synthetic capture test on staging tenant for baseline.
  - Verify backup of current agent config and bridge-proxy signing keys.
  - Confirm Immutable-store retention policies and available disk.
- Rolling upgrade steps (zero-downtime):
  1) Drain node A worker pool for bridge-proxy (k8s: cordon && drain).
  2) Deploy new bridge-proxy container (image tag).
  3) Smoke-test: bridge-proxy self-test endpoint /_health and a synthetic bundle upload.
  4) Uncordon node and proceed to next node.
  5) Staged agent-snap rollout: update DaemonSet with new image and a 30s pause between nodes for warm-cache verification.
- Rollback: revert DaemonSet and deployment images to previous tags. If manifest schema incompatible, Evidence Broker will reject reads; use the compatibility tool: evidence-cli reconcile --bundle <bundle_id> --downgrade-manifest.

Security and compliance considerations:
- All artifacts are signed and hashed; manifests include operator id, timestamp, and attestation token.
- Retention rules are enforced via Immutable-store lifecycle policies; customer-managed retention overrides Redwood defaults.
- Access control: RBAC enforced at Evidence Broker; retrieval requires two-person authorization for restricted datasets.
- For air-gapped/on-prem deployments: snapshot bundles remain on customer storage by default; Redwood-managed transfer is disabled unless customer enables egress and provides attestation.

Customer preflight & requirements checklist (operator-facing):
| Requirement | Minimum | Rationale |
|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Host disk staging | 10 GB per host | Temporary storage for bundle assembly and compression |
| Network egress | 10 Mbps sustained per bridge-proxy | Ensures bundle uploads during capture windows |
| KMS/HSM | Optional BYOK | Required for customers that restrict provider access |
| Time sync | NTP or equivalent, max skew 2s | Chain-of-custody timestamp correctness |
| Operator roles | 2 contacts with private-console access | For two-person approval flow |

Configuration examples (snippets):
- agent-snap capture window (k8s DaemonSet env):
  AGENT_CAPTURE_WINDOW=60 # seconds
  AGENT_MAX_STAGING_SIZE_MB=10240
- bridge-proxy config (yaml):
  encryption:
    mode: envelope
    kms_provider: customer-kms # options: customer-kms, redwood-kms
  upload:
    bucket: "tenant-forensics-prod"
    multipart_threshold_mb: 50

CLI examples:
- Initiate an evidence capture for tenant-123 for 120s via Evidence Broker CLI:
  evidence-cli capture --tenant tenant-123 --duration 120 --initiator jdoe@redwood.com
- Re-encrypt a failed bundle on bridge-proxy host:
  sudo bridge-proxyctl reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --force

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime team (primary): runtime-team@redwood.com — owner for agent-snap and runtime snapshot APIs.
- Observability team (secondary): observability@redwood.com — owner for dashboards and alerting.
- Security & Compliance: security-compliance@redwood.com — owner for chain-of-custody and retention policies.

Appendix A — Example manifest.json (illustrative):
{
  "bundle_id": "bndl-20260915-7f3a",
  "tenant_id": "tenant-123",
  "start_time": "2026-09-15T22:10:00Z",
  "end_time": "2026-09-15T22:12:00Z",
  "files": ["traces.ndjson.gz", "kvcache.snap.gz"],
  "hashes": {"traces.ndjson.gz": "<sha256>"},
  "signed_by": "bridge-proxy-1"
}

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions:
Q: Will enabling forensic capture increase costs?
A: Yes — storage, egress (if enabled), and operator time. We provide estimated per-1000-captures cost models during onboarding.

Q: Can customers choose which fields to redact?
A: Yes — redaction policy is configurable per-tenant and applied at agent-snap before bundle assembly. Redaction logs are included in the manifest.

Related artefacts and references:
- Runtime snapshot API spec: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/snapshot-api-spec-2025
- Immutable-store lifecycle policies: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/immutable-store-policies-2026
- Operator RBAC matrix: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/operator-rbac-matrix-2025

Revision history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-03: Added chain-of-custody signing details per security review.
- 2026-09-15: Published with BYOK and air-gap clarifications and additional runbooks.

title: Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge Reference Architecture
preview:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audits, and model-drift investigations. The design balances minimal performance impact, strong chain-of-custody guarantees, and customer privacy controls.

Goals:
- Provide an immutable, auditable snapshot pipeline for tenant state and request traces.
- Ensure snapshots can be collected with <=5% added tail-latency under normal load via asynchronous capture.
- Maintain tenant data isolation: all forensic snapshots are encrypted with customer KMS/HSM keys where required.
- Offer deterministic retention and evidence packaging for audits (customer-facing artifact).

When to use this architecture:
- Post-incident root cause analysis for private deployments where customer operators require local forensics.
- Regulatory evidence requests (e.g., finserv, healthcare) where full request-correspondence is needed.
- Model quality regression cases where sequence-level KV-caches and prefix traces are necessary to reproduce outputs.

High-level components:
1) Local Capture Agents (agent-snap): per-host process that performs non-blocking request tap, kv-cache snapshot coordination, and local staging.
2) Snapshot Bridge (bridge-proxy): tenant-scoped gateway that aggregates artifacts, signs bundles, and performs envelope encryption for transfer.
3) Forensic Store (immutable-store): append-only object store (S3-compatible) with versioned buckets and retention rules; may be hosted on customer object storage or Redwood-managed vault.
4) Evidence Broker (evidence-api): control-plane service to register retention policies, initiate snapshots, and produce audit manifests.
5) Operator Console (private console extension): UI + APIs for authorized operators to request, view, and package evidence bundles.

Architecture diagram (text):
- Tenant traffic -> Agent-snap (in host network namespace) -> local staging area -> Bridge-proxy (tenant egress to object store) -> Immutable-store (encrypted)
- Evidence Broker coordinates via control-plane channel (signed requests, attestation tokens)

Key design decisions and rationale:
- Asynchronous capture: Agent-snap never blocks request processing. Synchronous forcing would violate latency SLOs.
- Envelope encryption with customer KMS: ensures Redwood cannot read snapshots when customer requires BYOK.
- Minimal sensitive PII in bundled artifacts: by default we capture request-id, tokenized input/response, kv-cache state (configurable). Full plaintext capture requires explicit customer opt-in and attestation.

Detailed flow (step-by-step):
1) Trigger: Evidence request can be operator-initiated (console) or automated (alert rule). Evidence Broker validates the request, checks operator RBAC, and issues a short-lived signed attestation token to bridge-proxy.
2) Local Coordination: Agent-snap receives attestation token and enters \"capture window\" for the tenant (configurable 30s–10m). During the window it records non-blocking traces and initiates a consistent KV-cache snapshot across replicas using the runtime snapshot API.
3) Bundle creation: Agent-snap composes a manifest.json (metadata), a compressed trace file (.ndjson.gz), and a serialized kv-cache snapshot (.snap.gz). Each file is hashed (SHA-256) and recorded.
4) Signing & Encryption: Bridge-proxy validates attestation, signs the manifest with a bridge key, then performs envelope-encryption: file encrypted with data key; data key encrypted with customer KMS public key or Redwood-managed key depending on policy.
5) Upload & Index: Encrypted bundle uploaded to Immutable-store and indexed in Evidence Broker with metadata: tenant-id, originating host, time-range, SLOs at capture time, operator id. Evidence Broker generates an audit entry and exportable chain-of-custody record.
6) Retrieval: Authorized operators request retrieval. Evidence Broker verifies RBAC, returns decryption instructions (if Redwood-managed) or redirects to pre-signed customer storage download links (if BYOK), and records the retrieval event.

Operational SLOs and thresholds:
- Capture startup latency: agent-snap start < 200ms (metric: agent.capture.startup_ms P50/P95).
- Impact on tail latency: added P99 latency <= 5% of baseline for requests during capture windows.
- Upload success rate: 99.9% of bundles must reach Immutable-store within 2 minutes under normal network conditions.
- Chain-of-custody integrity: manifest and bundle signatures validated at time of retrieval or audit.

Alerting and monitoring (suggested):
- Alerts: agent.capture.failure_rate > 0.5% for 5m -> PagerDuty (oncall-owner: runtime-team)
- Alerts: bundle.upload.latency_p95 > 120s for 10m -> SRE team
- Dashboards: per-tenant capture activity, bundle sizes distribution, retention occupancy by customer, failed-encryption rates.

Operator runbooks (concise):
Runbook A — Investigate missing bundle:
1) Check Evidence Broker UI for \"bundle failed\" events. Note bundle_id.
2) SSH to originating host (use operator jumpbox): sudo journalctl -u agent-snap | grep bundle_id.
3) Validate local manifest and hash: sha256sum manifest.json -> compare with Evidence Broker index.
4) If encryption failed: check KMS connectivity (kms-proxy logs) and re-run bundle re-encrypt command: 
   bridge-proxy reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --retries 3
5) After fix, mark bundle re-uploaded in Evidence Broker and verify presence in Immutable-store.

Runbook B — Produce customer audit package:
1) Operator opens Evidence Broker -> select tenant -> time-range -> generate audit package.
2) Evidence Broker returns package_id and a pre-signed download link or decryption instructions.
3) For BYOK: instruct customer to perform local key unwrap and verify manifest signature.
4) Record the audit package delivery in customer QBR artifact store and notify the customer success lead.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (safe-to-execute checklist):
- Scope: agent-snap and bridge-proxy are the only services needing coordinated upgrade. Evidence Broker schema changes are backward-compatible.
- Preflight checks before upgrade:
  - Run synthetic capture test on staging tenant for baseline.
  - Verify backup of current agent config and bridge-proxy signing keys.
  - Confirm Immutable-store retention policies and available disk.
- Rolling upgrade steps (zero-downtime):
  1) Drain node A worker pool for bridge-proxy (k8s: cordon && drain).
  2) Deploy new bridge-proxy container (image tag).
  3) Smoke-test: bridge-proxy self-test endpoint /_health and a synthetic bundle upload.
  4) Uncordon node and proceed to next node.
  5) Staged agent-snap rollout: update DaemonSet with new image and a 30s pause between nodes for warm-cache verification.
- Rollback: revert DaemonSet and deployment images to previous tags. If manifest schema incompatible, Evidence Broker will reject reads; use the compatibility tool: evidence-cli reconcile --bundle <bundle_id> --downgrade-manifest.

Security and compliance considerations:
- All artifacts are signed and hashed; manifests include operator id, timestamp, and attestation token.
- Retention rules are enforced via Immutable-store lifecycle policies; customer-managed retention overrides Redwood defaults.
- Access control: RBAC enforced at Evidence Broker; retrieval requires two-person authorization for restricted datasets.
- For air-gapped/on-prem deployments: snapshot bundles remain on customer storage by default; Redwood-managed transfer is disabled unless customer enables egress and provides attestation.

Customer preflight & requirements checklist (operator-facing):
| Requirement | Minimum | Rationale |
|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Host disk staging | 10 GB per host | Temporary storage for bundle assembly and compression |
| Network egress | 10 Mbps sustained per bridge-proxy | Ensures bundle uploads during capture windows |
| KMS/HSM | Optional BYOK | Required for customers that restrict provider access |
| Time sync | NTP or equivalent, max skew 2s | Chain-of-custody timestamp correctness |
| Operator roles | 2 contacts with private-console access | For two-person approval flow |

Configuration examples (snippets):
- agent-snap capture window (k8s DaemonSet env):
  AGENT_CAPTURE_WINDOW=60 # seconds
  AGENT_MAX_STAGING_SIZE_MB=10240
- bridge-proxy config (yaml):
  encryption:
    mode: envelope
    kms_provider: customer-kms # options: customer-kms, redwood-kms
  upload:
    bucket: \"tenant-forensics-prod\"
    multipart_threshold_mb: 50

CLI examples:
- Initiate an evidence capture for tenant-123 for 120s via Evidence Broker CLI:
  evidence-cli capture --tenant tenant-123 --duration 120 --initiator jdoe@redwood.com
- Re-encrypt a failed bundle on bridge-proxy host:
  sudo bridge-proxyctl reencrypt --bundle <bundle_id> --force

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime team (primary): runtime-team@redwood.com — owner for agent-snap and runtime snapshot APIs.
- Observability team (secondary): observability@redwood.com — owner for dashboards and alerting.
- Security & Compliance: security-compliance@redwood.com — owner for chain-of-custody and retention policies.

Appendix A — Example manifest.json (illustrative):
{
  \"bundle_id\": \"bndl-20260915-7f3a\",
  \"tenant_id\": \"tenant-123\",
  \"start_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:10:00Z\",
  \"end_time\": \"2026-09-15T22:12:00Z\",
  \"files\": [\"traces.ndjson.gz\", \"kvcache.snap.gz\"],
  \"hashes\": {\"traces.ndjson.gz\": \"<sha256>\"},
  \"signed_by\": \"bridge-proxy-1\"
}

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions:
Q: Will enabling forensic capture increase costs?
A: Yes — storage, egress (if enabled), and operator time. We provide estimated per-1000-captures cost models during onboarding.

Q: Can customers choose which fields to redact?
A: Yes — redaction policy is configurable per-tenant and applied at agent-snap before bundle assembly. Redaction logs are included in the manifest.

Related artefacts and references:
- Runtime snapshot API spec: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/snapshot-api-spec-2025
- Immutable-store lifecycle policies: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/immutable-store-policies-2026
- Operator RBAC matrix: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/operator-rbac-matrix-2025
