source.title: VPC multitenant failure-domain hardened operator playbook
source.author: Leila Chen
source.body:
Summary:

This playbook defines operator procedures and hardened configuration recommendations to detect, contain, and recover from VPC-level failure domains in multitenant private deployments. It is intended for Redwood operators, field engineers, and SREs responsible for private VPC and on-prem tenant fleets.

Goals:
- Minimize customer-visible impact when a VPC-level failure (network partition, AZ outage, BGP flaps, service subnet corruption) occurs.
- Provide deterministic detection, containment, and rollback patterns that work across hosted, dedicated, and private control planes.
- Preserve tenant data integrity (KV caches, session state) and avoid cascading model-swap regressions.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: VPC-sited control/data plane failures that affect one or more tenants in a private deployment. Includes cross-VPC routing incidents, NAT/gateway failures, and service-subnet reachability loss.
- Audience: Field operators, on-call SREs, Private Deploy Ops, Customer Engineering. Not intended for pure hardware/BIOS-level recovery (see HW EOL guide).

Key terminology:
- Failure domain: a bounded set of hosts or network paths whose outage causes correlated tenant impact.
- Safemode: an automated degradation policy that reduces non-essential services (batching, telemetry sampling, model rollouts) to protect SLOs.
- KV sticky state: in-memory prefix/KV caches bound to model-serving processes; must be reconciled before cutovers.

High-level failure model and invariants:
1) Fail-stop network partitions (e.g., subnet route removed) should be isolated to a single failure domain; automated traffic steering must avoid spilling load to degraded domains.
2) Model swap/quantization operations must not be in-progress during a domain recovery; operator tooling must detect in-flight swaps.
3) Observable invariants: token latency p50/p95 increases by >2x, KV miss rate rises >15%, and model error/regression alerts trigger are strong indicators of a domain boundary breach.

Pre-deployment hardening checklist (table):
| Area | Required config | Rationale | Owner |
|------|-----------------|-----------|-------|
| VPC routing | BGP session keepalive 10s, bgp-multipath enabled, two transit gateways per region | Reduce single-BGP-session impact | Network team |
| Subnet layout | Service subnets paired across AZs, /26 minimum per service role | Limits blast radius | Infra |
| Control-plane proxies | Dual HA proxies with health-check TTL 5s | Fast failover for control-plane RPCs | Private Deploy Ops |
| KV persistence | Snapshot interval 60s for critical tenants, WAL retention 5x longer than upgrade window | Prevent state loss on eviction | Runtime team |
| Telemetry | Sampling 100% for error traces during incidents, token-level summaries every 30s | Faster triage | Observability team |

Configuration recommendations (concise):
- CIDR and routing: reserve a /20 per tenant-burst-block, implement strict subnet ACLs to prevent cross-tenant lateral movement.
- NAT gateway: deploy at least two NAT endpoints per AZ with dynamic route preference and health-based failover.
- Egress policing: apply tokenizable egress rate limits per tenant to prevent noisy neighbor amplification during repair.
- Control-plane endpoints: use TLS client cert authentication + mTLS between operator tools and control-plane with short-lived cert lifetimes (1h) for operator sessions during emergencies.

Operator guardrails and safemode triggers:
Safemode should be engaged automatically when any two of the following are true for a 3m window:
- p95 latency for text-generation API > 800ms and increased >2x from baseline
- KV cache miss rate > 15% sustained
- Drop in successful model response rate > 5% absolute
When safemode engages, the platform should: reduce batch sizes by 50%, disable non-critical telemetry streams, pin model variants to last-known-good, and refuse new model rollouts.

Incident detection play (quick triage):
1) Alert ingestion: triggers from the VPC router, NAT gateway, or control-plane health-checks alert the on-call.
2) Initial triage (first 5 minutes): confirm scope (single tenant vs. multiple tenants vs. full-VPC) using origin tags and tenant mapping.
3) Containment (10–20 minutes): attach traffic cordon to affected failure domain: remove route advertisement to external regions and route via transit gateway failover.

Step-by-step containment and recovery (operator runbook):
1) Classify incident: network-only | control-plane-only | mixed.
2) If network-only:
  a) Check BGP session state: `show bgp summary` and look for flaps.
  b) If BGP flap identified, increase hold-time to safe value and enable path damping for impacted session.
  c) Rehome NAT for affected subnets to alternate AZ NAT using `ip route` failover recipes.
3) If control-plane-only:
  a) Verify proxy health and restart proxies in degraded order: read-only proxies first, then read-write.
  b) Freeze model rollouts: `redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant` and confirm no in-flight artifacts.
4) Mixed incidents: escalate immediately to senior network + runtime engineer and run parallel steps above.
5) State reconciliation: rehydrate KV from last snapshot to a staging object store, run KV consistency check (sha256 checksums) and only reattach to live processes after verification.

Upgrade & rollback considerations during/after failures:
- Never perform stateful model swap or quantization during an active failure domain. Maintain an automated gate that checks: error budget consumption < 25% and no safemode engagement for 30m.
- Rollback recipe (if an upgrade caused the failure):
  1) Pin traffic to previous model tag using traffic-weighting: `redwoodctl traffic pin --model previous-tag --weight 100`
  2) Reconcile KV caches by performing a local warm-up run (seed 10% of common prefixes) before unfreezing rollouts.
  3) Run smoke generator across all tenants that saw the upgrade; compare token-level p50/p95 and rejection rates to baseline.

Testing and validation playbook (preproduction & field tests):
- Synthetic tests: run coordinated multi-tenant synthetic traffic with 10–30% tail-load spike to validate eviction windows.
- Chaos tests: scheduled chaos drills that simulate single-subnet route removal, NAT failure, and AZ probe loss. Use a canary tenant to validate rollback flow.
- Onboarding acceptance test (OAT): operator must pass a 3-hour injection window verification where safemode is exercised and recovery completed within RTO objective.

Telemetry and alerting (recommended sets and thresholds):
- Metrics to ingest: p50/p95/p99 latency per tenant, KV miss %, model error ratio, bgp session flaps per minute, NAT failed translations/sec.
- Alerts:
  - P95 latency > 800ms for 3m -> P1 to oncall (pager)
  - BGP session flaps > 5 in 2m -> P1 network paging
  - KV miss rate > 15% for 5m -> oncall slack + runbook link

Post-incident checklist and postmortem structure (within 48 hours):
1) Timeline reconstruction with delta-synchronized logs (include control-plane RPCs, NAT metrics, bgp summaries).
2) Root cause hypothesis and evidence table (logs, packet captures, traceroutes).
3) Mitigations applied, duration, customer impact (minutes, percent of calls failed).
4) Action items with owner and ETA.

Escalation matrix and ownership:
- First-line: Private Deploy Operators (oncall rotation)
- Second-line: Runtime Engineers (model/KV) and Network on-call
- Third-line: SRE Incident Commander + Customer Engineering for high-severity customer impact
- Contact procedures: use incident channel `#inc-private-vpc` and escalate to paging list if P1 after 10m.

Appendix A — Example CLI snippets:
- Freeze rollouts:
  ```
  redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant --tenant-id t-1234 --reason "vpc-failure-engaged"
  ```
- Pin traffic to previous model:
  ```
  redwoodctl traffic pin --tenant t-1234 --model v1.9.2 --weight 100
  ```
- KV snapshot and verification (conceptual):
  ```
  redwoodctl kv snapshot --tenant t-1234 --since "-2m" --out s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621
  redwoodctl kv verify --snapshot s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621 --sha256 <expected>
  ```

Appendix B — Example safemode YAML (operator-editable):
| key | value | description |
|-----|-------|-------------|
| safemode.enabled | true | master enable flag |
| safemode.triggers.latency_p95_ms | 800 | p95 threshold to evaluate |
| safemode.actions.reduce_batch_pct | 50 | how much to reduce batching |
| safemode.actions.freeze_rollout | true | prevents new model rollouts |
Example YAML snippet:
```
safemode:
  enabled: true
  triggers:
    latency_p95_ms: 800
    kv_miss_percent: 15
  actions:
    reduce_batch_pct: 50
    freeze_rollout: true
    telemetry_sampling: 0.1
```
Related references and reading:
- VPC tenant commissioning guide (operator handbook)
- Tenant eviction and safe-state sanitization playbook
- Regional model fallback topology (design doc)

Owner and contact:
- Document owner: Leila Chen (Private Deploy Ops) 
- Operational owner: Private Deploy SREs (oncall)

Revision history:
- 2026-02-15: created (Leila Chen)
- 2026-04-02: added safemode action set and triggers (Marco Alvarez)
- 2026-06-21: clarified KV snapshot verification and added rollback recipe (Priya Natarajan)

3) Containment (10–20 minutes): attach traffic cordon to affected failure domain: remove route advertisement to external regions and route via transit gateway failover.

Step-by-step containment and recovery (operator runbook):
1) Classify incident: network-only | control-plane-only | mixed.
2) If network-only:
  a) Check BGP session state: `show bgp summary` and look for flaps.
  b) If BGP flap identified, increase hold-time to safe value and enable path damping for impacted session.
  c) Rehome NAT for affected subnets to alternate AZ NAT using `ip route` failover recipes.
3) If control-plane-only:
  a) Verify proxy health and restart proxies in degraded order: read-only proxies first, then read-write.
  b) Freeze model rollouts: `redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant` and confirm no in-flight artifacts.
4) Mixed incidents: escalate immediately to senior network + runtime engineer and run parallel steps above.
5) State reconciliation: rehydrate KV from last snapshot to a staging object store, run KV consistency check (sha256 checksums) and only reattach to live processes after verification.

Upgrade & rollback considerations during/after failures:
- Never perform stateful model swap or quantization during an active failure domain. Maintain an automated gate that checks: error budget consumption < 25% and no safemode engagement for 30m.
- Rollback recipe (if an upgrade caused the failure):
  1) Pin traffic to previous model tag using traffic-weighting: `redwoodctl traffic pin --model previous-tag --weight 100`
  2) Reconcile KV caches by performing a local warm-up run (seed 10% of common prefixes) before unfreezing rollouts.
  3) Run smoke generator across all tenants that saw the upgrade; compare token-level p50/p95 and rejection rates to baseline.

Testing and validation playbook (preproduction & field tests):
- Synthetic tests: run coordinated multi-tenant synthetic traffic with 10–30% tail-load spike to validate eviction windows.
- Chaos tests: scheduled chaos drills that simulate single-subnet route removal, NAT failure, and AZ probe loss. Use a canary tenant to validate rollback flow.
- Onboarding acceptance test (OAT): operator must pass a 3-hour injection window verification where safemode is exercised and recovery completed within RTO objective.

Telemetry and alerting (recommended sets and thresholds):
- Metrics to ingest: p50/p95/p99 latency per tenant, KV miss %, model error ratio, bgp session flaps per minute, NAT failed translations/sec.
- Alerts:
  - P95 latency > 800ms for 3m -> P1 to oncall (pager)
  - BGP session flaps > 5 in 2m -> P1 network paging
  - KV miss rate > 15% for 5m -> oncall slack + runbook link

Post-incident checklist and postmortem structure (within 48 hours):
1) Timeline reconstruction with delta-synchronized logs (include control-plane RPCs, NAT metrics, bgp summaries).
2) Root cause hypothesis and evidence table (logs, packet captures, traceroutes).
3) Mitigations applied, duration, customer impact (minutes, percent of calls failed).
4) Action items with owner and ETA.

Escalation matrix and ownership:
- First-line: Private Deploy Operators (oncall rotation)
- Second-line: Runtime Engineers (model/KV) and Network on-call
- Third-line: SRE Incident Commander + Customer Engineering for high-severity customer impact
- Contact procedures: use incident channel `#inc-private-vpc` and escalate to paging list if P1 after 10m.

Appendix A — Example CLI snippets:
- Freeze rollouts:
  ```
  redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant --tenant-id t-1234 --reason \"vpc-failure-engaged\"
  ```
- Pin traffic to previous model:
  ```
  redwoodctl traffic pin --tenant t-1234 --model v1.9.2 --weight 100
  ```
- KV snapshot and verification (conceptual):
  ```
  redwoodctl kv snapshot --tenant t-1234 --since \"-2m\" --out s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621
  redwoodctl kv verify --snapshot s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621 --sha256 <expected>
  ```

Appendix B — Example safemode YAML (operator-editable):
| key | value | description |
|-----|-------|-------------|
| safemode.enabled | true | master enable flag |
| safemode.triggers.latency_p95_ms | 800 | p95 threshold to evaluate |
| safemode.actions.reduce_batch_pct | 50 | how much to reduce batching |
| safemode.actions.freeze_rollout | true | prevents new model rollouts |
Example YAML snippet:
```
safemode:
  enabled: true
  triggers:
    latency_p95_ms: 800
    kv_miss_percent: 15
  actions:
    reduce_batch_pct: 50
    freeze_rollout: true
    telemetry_sampling: 0.1
```
Related references and reading:
- VPC tenant commissioning guide (operator handbook)
- Tenant eviction and safe-state sanitization playbook
- Regional model fallback topology (design doc)

Owner and contact:
- Document owner: Leila Chen (Private Deploy Ops) 
- Operational owner: Private Deploy SREs (oncall)

Revision history:
- 2026-02-15: created (Leila Chen)
- 2026-04-02: added safemode action set and triggers (Marco Alvarez)
- 2026-06-21: clarified KV snapshot verification and added rollback recipe (Priya Natarajan)

Summary:

This playbook defines operator procedures and hardened configuration recommendations to detect, contain, and recover from VPC-level failure domains in multitenant private deployments. It is intended for Redwood operators, field engineers, and SREs responsible for private VPC and on-prem tenant fleets.

Goals:
- Minimize customer-visible impact when a VPC-level failure (network partition, AZ outage, BGP flaps, service subnet corruption) occurs.
- Provide deterministic detection, containment, and rollback patterns that work across hosted, dedicated, and private control planes.
- Preserve tenant data integrity (KV caches, session state) and avoid cascading model-swap regressions.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: VPC-sited control/data plane failures that affect one or more tenants in a private deployment. Includes cross-VPC routing incidents, NAT/gateway failures, and service-subnet reachability loss.
- Audience: Field operators, on-call SREs, Private Deploy Ops, Customer Engineering. Not intended for pure hardware/BIOS-level recovery (see HW EOL guide).

Key terminology:
- Failure domain: a bounded set of hosts or network paths whose outage causes correlated tenant impact.
- Safemode: an automated degradation policy that reduces non-essential services (batching, telemetry sampling, model rollouts) to protect SLOs.
- KV sticky state: in-memory prefix/KV caches bound to model-serving processes; must be reconciled before cutovers.

High-level failure model and invariants:
1) Fail-stop network partitions (e.g., subnet route removed) should be isolated to a single failure domain; automated traffic steering must avoid spilling load to degraded domains.
2) Model swap/quantization operations must not be in-progress during a domain recovery; operator tooling must detect in-flight swaps.
3) Observable invariants: token latency p50/p95 increases by >2x, KV miss rate rises >15%, and model error/regression alerts trigger are strong indicators of a domain boundary breach.

Pre-deployment hardening checklist (table):
| Area | Required config | Rationale | Owner |
|------|-----------------|-----------|-------|
| VPC routing | BGP session keepalive 10s, bgp-multipath enabled, two transit gateways per region | Reduce single-BGP-session impact | Network team |
| Subnet layout | Service subnets paired across AZs, /26 minimum per service role | Limits blast radius | Infra |
| Control-plane proxies | Dual HA proxies with health-check TTL 5s | Fast failover for control-plane RPCs | Private Deploy Ops |
| KV persistence | Snapshot interval 60s for critical tenants, WAL retention 5x longer than upgrade window | Prevent state loss on eviction | Runtime team |
| Telemetry | Sampling 100% for error traces during incidents, token-level summaries every 30s | Faster triage | Observability team |

Configuration recommendations (concise):
- CIDR and routing: reserve a /20 per tenant-burst-block, implement strict subnet ACLs to prevent cross-tenant lateral movement.
- NAT gateway: deploy at least two NAT endpoints per AZ with dynamic route preference and health-based failover.
- Egress policing: apply tokenizable egress rate limits per tenant to prevent noisy neighbor amplification during repair.
- Control-plane endpoints: use TLS client cert authentication + mTLS between operator tools and control-plane with short-lived cert lifetimes (1h) for operator sessions during emergencies.

Operator guardrails and safemode triggers:
Safemode should be engaged automatically when any two of the following are true for a 3m window:
- p95 latency for text-generation API > 800ms and increased >2x from baseline
- KV cache miss rate > 15% sustained
- Drop in successful model response rate > 5% absolute
When safemode engages, the platform should: reduce batch sizes by 50%, disable non-critical telemetry streams, pin model variants to last-known-good, and refuse new model rollouts.

Incident detection play (quick triage):
1) Alert ingestion: triggers from the VPC router, NAT gateway, or control-plane health-checks alert the on-call.
2) Initial triage (first 5 minutes): confirm scope (single tenant vs. multiple tenants vs. full-VPC) using origin tags and tenant mapping.
3) Containment (10–20 minutes): attach traffic cordon to affected failure domain: remove route advertisement to external regions and route via transit gateway failover.

Step-by-step containment and recovery (operator runbook):
1) Classify incident: network-only | control-plane-only | mixed.
2) If network-only:
  a) Check BGP session state: `show bgp summary` and look for flaps.
  b) If BGP flap identified, increase hold-time to safe value and enable path damping for impacted session.
  c) Rehome NAT for affected subnets to alternate AZ NAT using `ip route` failover recipes.
3) If control-plane-only:
  a) Verify proxy health and restart proxies in degraded order: read-only proxies first, then read-write.
  b) Freeze model rollouts: `redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant` and confirm no in-flight artifacts.
4) Mixed incidents: escalate immediately to senior network + runtime engineer and run parallel steps above.
5) State reconciliation: rehydrate KV from last snapshot to a staging object store, run KV consistency check (sha256 checksums) and only reattach to live processes after verification.

Upgrade & rollback considerations during/after failures:
- Never perform stateful model swap or quantization during an active failure domain. Maintain an automated gate that checks: error budget consumption < 25% and no safemode engagement for 30m.
- Rollback recipe (if an upgrade caused the failure):
  1) Pin traffic to previous model tag using traffic-weighting: `redwoodctl traffic pin --model previous-tag --weight 100`
  2) Reconcile KV caches by performing a local warm-up run (seed 10% of common prefixes) before unfreezing rollouts.
  3) Run smoke generator across all tenants that saw the upgrade; compare token-level p50/p95 and rejection rates to baseline.

Testing and validation playbook (preproduction & field tests):
- Synthetic tests: run coordinated multi-tenant synthetic traffic with 10–30% tail-load spike to validate eviction windows.
- Chaos tests: scheduled chaos drills that simulate single-subnet route removal, NAT failure, and AZ probe loss. Use a canary tenant to validate rollback flow.
- Onboarding acceptance test (OAT): operator must pass a 3-hour injection window verification where safemode is exercised and recovery completed within RTO objective.

Telemetry and alerting (recommended sets and thresholds):
- Metrics to ingest: p50/p95/p99 latency per tenant, KV miss %, model error ratio, bgp session flaps per minute, NAT failed translations/sec.
- Alerts:
  - P95 latency > 800ms for 3m -> P1 to oncall (pager)
  - BGP session flaps > 5 in 2m -> P1 network paging
  - KV miss rate > 15% for 5m -> oncall slack + runbook link

Post-incident checklist and postmortem structure (within 48 hours):
1) Timeline reconstruction with delta-synchronized logs (include control-plane RPCs, NAT metrics, bgp summaries).
2) Root cause hypothesis and evidence table (logs, packet captures, traceroutes).
3) Mitigations applied, duration, customer impact (minutes, percent of calls failed).
4) Action items with owner and ETA.

Escalation matrix and ownership:
- First-line: Private Deploy Operators (oncall rotation)
- Second-line: Runtime Engineers (model/KV) and Network on-call
- Third-line: SRE Incident Commander + Customer Engineering for high-severity customer impact
- Contact procedures: use incident channel `#inc-private-vpc` and escalate to paging list if P1 after 10m.

Appendix A — Example CLI snippets:
- Freeze rollouts:
  ```
  redwoodctl rollout freeze --scope=tenant --tenant-id t-1234 --reason \"vpc-failure-engaged\"
  ```
- Pin traffic to previous model:
  ```
  redwoodctl traffic pin --tenant t-1234 --model v1.9.2 --weight 100
  ```
- KV snapshot and verification (conceptual):
  ```
  redwoodctl kv snapshot --tenant t-1234 --since \"-2m\" --out s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621
  redwoodctl kv verify --snapshot s3://private-artifacts/t-1234/snap-20260621 --sha256 <expected>
  ```

Appendix B — Example safemode YAML (operator-editable):
| key | value | description |
|-----|-------|-------------|
| safemode.enabled | true | master enable flag |
| safemode.triggers.latency_p95_ms | 800 | p95 threshold to evaluate |
| safemode.actions.reduce_batch_pct | 50 | how much to reduce batching |
| safemode.actions.freeze_rollout | true | prevents new model rollouts |
Example YAML snippet:
```
safemode:
  enabled: true
  triggers:
    latency_p95_ms: 800
    kv_miss_percent: 15
  actions:
    reduce_batch_pct: 50
    freeze_rollout: true
    telemetry_sampling: 0.1
```
Related references and reading:

- Tenant eviction and safe-state sanitization playbook
- Regional model fallback topology (design doc)

- Operational owner: Private Deploy SREs (oncall)

source.title: Observability Contracts and Log Sampling Strategy for Private Deploys
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview
This document defines the observability contract, telemetry sampling strategy, and retention policy recommended for Redwood private deployments (VPC and on-prem) to balance operational fidelity, privacy, and cost. It is intended for SREs, field engineers, operator teams, and customer success during onboarding and day-2 operations.

Goals
- Provide clear, implementable telemetry gates customers must satisfy for production acceptance.
- Minimize data transfer and storage costs while preserving the ability to diagnose P1/P2 incidents.
- Ensure telemetry respects customer privacy constraints and data residency requirements.
- Standardize sampling and log retention so our support and engineering teams can reliably interpret signals across customers.

Audience and scope
- Applies to private deployments managed by Redwood (hybrid control plane) and air-gapped on-prem packages where telemetry is permitted.
- Does NOT mandate vendor-specific agents; instead it specifies data contracts and functional requirements.

Observability contract (high-level)
Each private deployment must implement these telemetry categories and minimum signal quality. If a customer opts out of any category for compliance reasons, a formal exception must be recorded and compensated by increased local retention/windowing for troubleshooting.

Required signal categories and SLA coverage
1) Control-plane telemetry (mandatory unless restricted):
   - Heartbeats: 1s-5s cadence per node/service.
   - Lease and leader election events: emitable on transition.
   - Control-plane traces: sampling 100% for error traces, 0.5% for success traces by default.
2) Serving runtime telemetry (mandatory):
   - Request/response latency histograms (P50/P90/P99) per model, per route, per GPU-pool.
   - Token throughput and batch size distribution.
   - KV-cache hit/miss counters and eviction rates.
   - Per-request error classification (model, runtime, infra).
3) Resource and host metrics (mandatory):
   - GPU utilization, temperature, memory alloc/free, PCIe bandwidth (1s-10s resolution).
   - Disk IOPS and latency for shared storage.
4) Structured application logs (required with sampling):
   - Logs must include a structured correlation_id, tenant_id (hashed if required), route, and model_version.
5) Audit events (always required if customer permits):
   - Model pull/push, image signatures, config changes, and KMS key rotations.

Minimum SLOs for telemetry availability
- Heartbeat availability: 99.9% (30d window).
- Metric freshness: 30s for critical metrics, 5m for non-critical.
- Trace indexing latency (where remote indexing is used): < 5 minutes for sampled traces.

Retention and privacy requirements
- Default retention: 30 days for metrics, 90 days for structured logs at low sampling, 365 days for aggregated rollups (daily/hourly).
- For customers with strict data residency, telemetry must be region-locked and stored using the customer-managed storage endpoint or removed after a configurable TTL.
- PII/PHI: no plaintext customer content or unredacted prompts may leave the customer environment. If request payloads are necessary for deep debug, they must be redacted and transmitted only with explicit customer approval and documented evidence.

Sampling strategy (principles)
- Diagnose-first: sampling must preserve high-fidelity signals for errors, anomalous latencies, and low-frequency safety/regression events.
- Budget-aware: sampling rates must be adjustable by operator to control egress and storage costs.
- Deterministic sharding: when sampling by tenant, use a stable hash of tenant_id to ensure consistent retention windows for a tenant over time.

Recommended sampling knobs and defaults
- Error traces: sample_rate = 100% (always capture)
- High-latency requests (latency > P99 baseline): sample_rate = 100%
- Normal successful requests: sample_rate = 0.5% (default)
- Safety or eval-triggered requests (if flagged): sample_rate = 100%
- Debug mode (operator-triggered for tenant or route): sample_rate = 100% for the duration of the debug window (recommended max 24 hours)
- Log-level sampling: capture ALL error logs, 10% of WARN logs, 1% of INFO logs by default

Algorithms and implementation notes
- Use reservoir or head-based sampling for traces where high-cardinality services can flood collectors. Prefer head-based deterministic sampling keyed by trace_id for trace continuity.
- For logs, implement probabilistic sampling at the logging agent but allow server-side overrides for error/warn levels.
- Implement a rolling adaptive sampler: if the local egress budget for a tenant is exceeded for a window, degrade INFO sampling first, then WARN, never error.

Configuration examples
- Example YAML snippet (agent-side):

```yaml
telemetry:
  metrics:
    flush_interval: 15s
    critical_resolution: 10s
  traces:
    error_sample_rate: 1.0
    success_sample_rate: 0.005
    latency_threshold_ms: 1500
  logs:
    levels:
      ERROR: 1.0
      WARN: 0.10
      INFO: 0.01
    redact_patterns:
      - 'Authorization: .*'
      - 'Bearer .*'
    tenant_shard_hash: 'fnv1a64'
  egress_budget_per_day_mb: 1024
```

- Example rollout CLI commands for operators (where a remote collector exists):
  - Verify config: redwoodctl telemetry validate --config ./telemetry.yaml
  - Stage config: redwoodctl telemetry stage --env prod --file ./telemetry.yaml
  - Activate: redwoodctl telemetry activate --env prod --timeout 600s

Acceptance tests and smoke checks
1) Freshness runbook (operator checklist):
   - Deploy config to a canary node.
   - Generate synthetic load (mixed latencies) for 15 minutes.
   - Assert metrics appear in collector within 30s for critical metrics.
   - Assert sampled traces for at least 1 in 200 successful requests and all error traces present.
2) Privacy compliance run:
   - Run automated scanner over outgoing telemetry to confirm redact_patterns are applied.
   - Confirm no payloads matching prompt-patterns are transmitted unless explicitly allowed.
3) Cost gate tests:
   - Simulate peak workload for 2h; measure egress. Ensure egress <= egress_budget_per_day_mb scaled over window.

Operational runbook (day-2)
- Alerts to configure (examples):
  - Telemetry egress exceeded for tenant X: p90 of last 1h > budget threshold -> page oncall-sre.
  - Collector backlog > 5m: trigger degraded sampling policy and notify field-op.
  - Heartbeats missing on > 3 nodes in a GPU-pool for 60s -> run node-fence and escalate.

- Troubleshooting steps (common scenarios):
  1) Missing metrics: verify agent status, agent TLS certs, network egress path to collector, and disk pressure.
  2) No traces for errors: check trace instrumentation level, ensure trace headers are propagated through proxies, and confirm error sampling override is not accidentally disabled.
  3) Sudden egress spike: identify route/tenant using deterministic shard sampling logs and temporarily escalate sampling limits for internal triage.

Rollout and change control
- All telemetry configuration changes must follow the change-management template in /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management and include:
  - Impact analysis (expected egress and cost delta),
  - Privacy impact (what fields could be leaked),
  - Rollback steps for immediate removal,
  - Stakeholder sign-off (customer success if customer-managed).
- Use canary strategy: 1% of nodes -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with 30m-2h observation windows depending on change scope.

Ownership and contacts
- Default owners: Observability Platform (team: eng-platform/observability).
- Field owner: Private Deployments Field-ops (team: eng-private-deployments).
- Escalation: oncall-observability -> oncall-sre -> eng-platform lead.

Appendix: quick reference table (text)
| Signal | Default retention | Default sampling | Notes |
| Heartbeats | 7d | 100% | High volume, short retention |
| Metrics (raw) | 30d | 100% | Rolled-up to 365d |
| Traces (error) | 90d | 100% | Export on demand if longer needed |
| Traces (success) | 30d | 0.5% | Adjustable |
| Logs (error) | 90d | 100% | Structured and indexed |
| Logs (info) | 30d | 1% | Low cardinality only |

Related artifacts and links
- Sample ingestion schema: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/reference-architectures/telemetry-ingest-aggregation-patterns-private-deploys-2026
- Post-install telemetry checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Change management: /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management/change-control-template

Revision history
- 2026-02-15: document created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-10: added adaptive sampling guidance and adaptive egress gate.
- 2026-06-01: clarified privacy/redaction rules and added CLI examples.

End of document

  3) Sudden egress spike: identify route/tenant using deterministic shard sampling logs and temporarily escalate sampling limits for internal triage.

Rollout and change control
- All telemetry configuration changes must follow the change-management template in /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management and include:
  - Impact analysis (expected egress and cost delta),
  - Privacy impact (what fields could be leaked),
  - Rollback steps for immediate removal,
  - Stakeholder sign-off (customer success if customer-managed).
- Use canary strategy: 1% of nodes -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with 30m-2h observation windows depending on change scope.

Ownership and contacts
- Default owners: Observability Platform (team: eng-platform/observability).
- Field owner: Private Deployments Field-ops (team: eng-private-deployments).
- Escalation: oncall-observability -> oncall-sre -> eng-platform lead.

Appendix: quick reference table (text)
| Signal | Default retention | Default sampling | Notes |
| Heartbeats | 7d | 100% | High volume, short retention |
| Metrics (raw) | 30d | 100% | Rolled-up to 365d |
| Traces (error) | 90d | 100% | Export on demand if longer needed |
| Traces (success) | 30d | 0.5% | Adjustable |
| Logs (error) | 90d | 100% | Structured and indexed |
| Logs (info) | 30d | 1% | Low cardinality only |

Related artifacts and links
- Sample ingestion schema: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/reference-architectures/telemetry-ingest-aggregation-patterns-private-deploys-2026
- Post-install telemetry checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Change management: /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management/change-control-template

Revision history
- 2026-02-15: document created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-10: added adaptive sampling guidance and adaptive egress gate.
- 2026-06-01: clarified privacy/redaction rules and added CLI examples.

End of document
text:
Overview
This document defines the observability contract, telemetry sampling strategy, and retention policy recommended for Redwood private deployments (VPC and on-prem) to balance operational fidelity, privacy, and cost. It is intended for SREs, field engineers, operator teams, and customer success during onboarding and day-2 operations.

Goals
- Provide clear, implementable telemetry gates customers must satisfy for production acceptance.
- Minimize data transfer and storage costs while preserving the ability to diagnose P1/P2 incidents.
- Ensure telemetry respects customer privacy constraints and data residency requirements.
- Standardize sampling and log retention so our support and engineering teams can reliably interpret signals across customers.

Audience and scope
- Applies to private deployments managed by Redwood (hybrid control plane) and air-gapped on-prem packages where telemetry is permitted.
- Does NOT mandate vendor-specific agents; instead it specifies data contracts and functional requirements.

Observability contract (high-level)
Each private deployment must implement these telemetry categories and minimum signal quality. If a customer opts out of any category for compliance reasons, a formal exception must be recorded and compensated by increased local retention/windowing for troubleshooting.

Required signal categories and SLA coverage
1) Control-plane telemetry (mandatory unless restricted):
   - Heartbeats: 1s-5s cadence per node/service.
   - Lease and leader election events: emitable on transition.
   - Control-plane traces: sampling 100% for error traces, 0.5% for success traces by default.
2) Serving runtime telemetry (mandatory):
   - Request/response latency histograms (P50/P90/P99) per model, per route, per GPU-pool.
   - Token throughput and batch size distribution.
   - KV-cache hit/miss counters and eviction rates.
   - Per-request error classification (model, runtime, infra).
3) Resource and host metrics (mandatory):
   - GPU utilization, temperature, memory alloc/free, PCIe bandwidth (1s-10s resolution).
   - Disk IOPS and latency for shared storage.
4) Structured application logs (required with sampling):
   - Logs must include a structured correlation_id, tenant_id (hashed if required), route, and model_version.
5) Audit events (always required if customer permits):
   - Model pull/push, image signatures, config changes, and KMS key rotations.

Minimum SLOs for telemetry availability
- Heartbeat availability: 99.9% (30d window).
- Metric freshness: 30s for critical metrics, 5m for non-critical.
- Trace indexing latency (where remote indexing is used): < 5 minutes for sampled traces.

Retention and privacy requirements
- Default retention: 30 days for metrics, 90 days for structured logs at low sampling, 365 days for aggregated rollups (daily/hourly).
- For customers with strict data residency, telemetry must be region-locked and stored using the customer-managed storage endpoint or removed after a configurable TTL.
- PII/PHI: no plaintext customer content or unredacted prompts may leave the customer environment. If request payloads are necessary for deep debug, they must be redacted and transmitted only with explicit customer approval and documented evidence.

Sampling strategy (principles)
- Diagnose-first: sampling must preserve high-fidelity signals for errors, anomalous latencies, and low-frequency safety/regression events.
- Budget-aware: sampling rates must be adjustable by operator to control egress and storage costs.
- Deterministic sharding: when sampling by tenant, use a stable hash of tenant_id to ensure consistent retention windows for a tenant over time.

Recommended sampling knobs and defaults
- Error traces: sample_rate = 100% (always capture)
- High-latency requests (latency > P99 baseline): sample_rate = 100%
- Normal successful requests: sample_rate = 0.5% (default)
- Safety or eval-triggered requests (if flagged): sample_rate = 100%
- Debug mode (operator-triggered for tenant or route): sample_rate = 100% for the duration of the debug window (recommended max 24 hours)
- Log-level sampling: capture ALL error logs, 10% of WARN logs, 1% of INFO logs by default

Algorithms and implementation notes
- Use reservoir or head-based sampling for traces where high-cardinality services can flood collectors. Prefer head-based deterministic sampling keyed by trace_id for trace continuity.
- For logs, implement probabilistic sampling at the logging agent but allow server-side overrides for error/warn levels.
- Implement a rolling adaptive sampler: if the local egress budget for a tenant is exceeded for a window, degrade INFO sampling first, then WARN, never error.

Configuration examples
- Example YAML snippet (agent-side):

```yaml
telemetry:
  metrics:
    flush_interval: 15s
    critical_resolution: 10s
  traces:
    error_sample_rate: 1.0
    success_sample_rate: 0.005
    latency_threshold_ms: 1500
  logs:
    levels:
      ERROR: 1.0
      WARN: 0.10
      INFO: 0.01
    redact_patterns:
      - 'Authorization: .*'
      - 'Bearer .*'
    tenant_shard_hash: 'fnv1a64'
  egress_budget_per_day_mb: 1024
```

- Example rollout CLI commands for operators (where a remote collector exists):
  - Verify config: redwoodctl telemetry validate --config ./telemetry.yaml
  - Stage config: redwoodctl telemetry stage --env prod --file ./telemetry.yaml
  - Activate: redwoodctl telemetry activate --env prod --timeout 600s

Acceptance tests and smoke checks
1) Freshness runbook (operator checklist):
   - Deploy config to a canary node.
   - Generate synthetic load (mixed latencies) for 15 minutes.
   - Assert metrics appear in collector within 30s for critical metrics.
   - Assert sampled traces for at least 1 in 200 successful requests and all error traces present.
2) Privacy compliance run:
   - Run automated scanner over outgoing telemetry to confirm redact_patterns are applied.
   - Confirm no payloads matching prompt-patterns are transmitted unless explicitly allowed.
3) Cost gate tests:
   - Simulate peak workload for 2h; measure egress. Ensure egress <= egress_budget_per_day_mb scaled over window.

Operational runbook (day-2)
- Alerts to configure (examples):
  - Telemetry egress exceeded for tenant X: p90 of last 1h > budget threshold -> page oncall-sre.
  - Collector backlog > 5m: trigger degraded sampling policy and notify field-op.
  - Heartbeats missing on > 3 nodes in a GPU-pool for 60s -> run node-fence and escalate.

- Troubleshooting steps (common scenarios):
  1) Missing metrics: verify agent status, agent TLS certs, network egress path to collector, and disk pressure.
  2) No traces for errors: check trace instrumentation level, ensure trace headers are propagated through proxies, and confirm error sampling override is not accidentally disabled.
  3) Sudden egress spike: identify route/tenant using deterministic shard sampling logs and temporarily escalate sampling limits for internal triage.

Rollout and change control
- All telemetry configuration changes must follow the change-management template in /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management and include:
  - Impact analysis (expected egress and cost delta),
  - Privacy impact (what fields could be leaked),
  - Rollback steps for immediate removal,
  - Stakeholder sign-off (customer success if customer-managed).
- Use canary strategy: 1% of nodes -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with 30m-2h observation windows depending on change scope.

Ownership and contacts
- Default owners: Observability Platform (team: eng-platform/observability).
- Field owner: Private Deployments Field-ops (team: eng-private-deployments).
- Escalation: oncall-observability -> oncall-sre -> eng-platform lead.

Appendix: quick reference table (text)
| Signal | Default retention | Default sampling | Notes |
| Heartbeats | 7d | 100% | High volume, short retention |
| Metrics (raw) | 30d | 100% | Rolled-up to 365d |
| Traces (error) | 90d | 100% | Export on demand if longer needed |
| Traces (success) | 30d | 0.5% | Adjustable |
| Logs (error) | 90d | 100% | Structured and indexed |
| Logs (info) | 30d | 1% | Low cardinality only |

Related artifacts and links
- Sample ingestion schema: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/reference-architectures/telemetry-ingest-aggregation-patterns-private-deploys-2026
- Post-install telemetry checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Change management: /confluence/policies-and-process/change-management/change-control-template

Revision history
- 2026-02-15: document created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2026-04-10: added adaptive sampling guidance and adaptive egress gate.
- 2026-06-01: clarified privacy/redaction rules and added CLI examples.

End of document

source.title: Field Operator Offline Debugging & Synthetic SLA Replay Playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview:

This playbook describes the standardized process for field operators and on-call engineers to perform offline debugging of private-deploy runtime incidents and to run deterministic synthetic SLA replay tests. Use this when a customer site experiences intermittent or reproducible SLA regressions but live traffic replay is not possible or desirable (air-gapped sites, PII constraints, or when reproducing production load would risk further degradation).

Goals:
- Produce a deterministic reproduction of the failing condition with minimal customer impact.
- Validate mitigation and rollout changes by running synthetic SLA replays against a controlled replica of the tenant environment.
- Capture forensics and telemetry artifacts required for post-incident review and compliance evidence.

When to use this playbook:
- Repeated elevated p95/p99 latency for inference endpoints with no clear live-trigger-able pattern.
- Tenant-facing degradation that cannot be safely reproduced with live traffic (regulated customers, air-gapped installs).
- After a model swap, quantization change, or runtime upgrade that correlates with latency or error regression.

Preconditions (must be satisfied before starting):
1) Operator prerequisites:
   - Operator role: tenant-operator or field-operator (RBAC token present).
   - Local toolkit: redwood-agent v2.4+ installed and authenticated (run redwood-agent whoami).
2) Telemetry and snapshot prerequisites:
   - Telemetry sampling level must include full request/response traces for target tenant (eg: sampling=0.1 or full for timestamp window).
   - A recent state snapshot of model artifacts, KV cache metadata, and runtime config exported to the safe bucket (see Snapshot naming convention below).
3) Safety and approvals:
   - For customer-facing private deployments, secure approval from the customer point-of-contact (email or ticket) is required unless the tenant is in a pre-agreed emergency escalation policy.

Key artifacts to collect before offline replay:
- runtime-metrics-export-<tenant>-<ts>.tgz (CPU/GPU utilization, host-level metrics)
- kv-cache-metadata-<tenant>-<ts>.json (KV cache size, hit/miss rates)
- model-artifact-manifest-<tenant>-<version>.yaml (model checksum, quantization profile)
- request-sample-set-<tenant>-<window>.jsonl (sanitized inputs for replay)

Snapshot naming convention (example):
- <tenant>-snapshot-2026-03-14T14-12-00Z

Environment setup (operator steps):
1) Provision an isolated replay environment (operator sandbox) that mirrors the tenant runtime:
   - Create an ephemeral namespace: redwood-<tenant>-replay-<run-id>
   - Clone control-plane config: redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --to ./config.yaml
   - Ensure model artifacts are mounted read-only from the snapshot bundle.
2) Configure telemetry to capture a full replay trace:
   - redwood-telemetry set sampling --tenant <tenant> --level full --target redis

Synthetic SLA replay specification (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Purpose |
|---|---:|---:|
| duration | 15m | Validate sustained behavior over steady-state window |
| rps | 50 | Start at low RPS then scale (see ramp schedule) |
| concurrency | 8 | Parallel client threads to stress batching paths |
| payload-distribution | 70% short (<=64 tokens), 30% long (>=512 tokens) | Matches observed production token distribution |
| seed | fixed per run-id | Ensure deterministic model sampling when applicable |

Example synthetic workload YAML (paste into replay controller):
```yaml
run_id: replay-2026-05-02-opsA
tenant: example-corp
duration: 900 # seconds
ramp_schedule:
  - { duration: 120, start_rps: 5, end_rps: 20 }
  - { duration: 300, start_rps: 20, end_rps: 50 }
concurrency: 8
payloads: ./request-sample-set-example.jsonl
token_distribution:
  short: 0.7
  long: 0.3
metrics_export: ./replay-metrics/
```

Replay runbook (step-by-step):
1) Validate preconditions and collect pre-run artifacts (see above).
2) Start telemetry capture: redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id>
3) Boot ephemeral replay environment (operator sandbox). Confirm host and GPU topology match production tenant profile (use redwoodctl topology --tenant <tenant>).
4) Load sanitized request-sample-set and configure the replay controller with the run YAML.
5) Start the run in dry-run mode (no model execution) to verify request timing and sampling: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --dry-run.
6) When dry-run succeeds, start an instrumented run: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>.
7) Monitor SLOs live: dashboard link: https://console.internal/redwood/replay/<run-id> (CPU/GPU, queue-latency, KV hit-rate, per-model p95/p99).
8) If observed p95/p99 breaches configured acceptance gates (see Safety gates below), abort and capture full artifacts.
9) If run is stable, iterate: adjust model quantization profile or batching window and re-run to validate improvements.

Acceptance gates and abort criteria (safety):
- Abort immediately if:
  - Error rate increases > 5x baseline and absolute error rate > 1%
  - GPU OOM observed on >1 node during run
  - Host-level thermal emergency or fan-throttle event
- Soft gate: flag run if p99 latency increases by >25% vs baseline for more than 2 consecutive sampling windows (1 minute each).

Post-run forensic collection checklist (automated + manual):
1) redwood-telemetry stop --label replay-<run-id> && redwood-telemetry bundle --label replay-<run-id> --out ./telemetry-<run-id>.tgz
2) Export replay metrics: redwood-replay export-metrics --label replay-<run-id> --format prometheus --out ./metrics-<run-id>.prom
3) Capture model runtime profiler snapshot: redwood-profiler snapshot --label replay-<run-id> --out profiler-<run-id>.json
4) Package all artifacts and upload to secure evidence bucket with run metadata: s3://evidence.redwood/replays/<tenant>/<run-id>/

Rollback and mitigation recommendations (if replay confirms regression):
- Short-term (immediate):
  1) Revert model variant to last-known-good model-artifact-manifest (model swap rollback)
  2) Reduce batching window to force lower queueing latency (config: runtime.batch.window_ms -> 0-5ms)
  3) Throttle tenant RPS via edge-router for controlled traffic shed (policy: tenant-traffic-quota)
- Mid-term:
  1) Apply targeted KV cache warm-up script prior to traffic ramp (see Appendix)
  2) Roll forward a quantization fallback variant (configured in runtime catalog with label quant-fallback)

Owner and escalation matrix:
- Primary: Field Operations (field-ops@redwood.com)
- Secondary: Serving Runtime Team (runtime@redwood.com)
- Escalation to Engineering Manager (on-call): esc-runtime@redwood.com if no mitigation within 2 hours.

SLOs and measurement window for replay validation:
- p95 inference latency target: <= 1200ms for long requests; <= 120ms for short requests
- p99 inference latency target: <= 2500ms for long requests; <= 350ms for short requests
- KV cache hit-rate: >= 85% during steady state (otherwise investigate eviction policy)

Operational notes and best practices:
- Always use sanitized request samples for regulated tenants. Strip any PII and validate with the customer data owner before replay.
- Use the same model seed and deterministic sampling options in the runtime to make replays repeatable. Set RNG seed in config: runtime.model.seed: <run-seed>.
- For air-gapped installs, use the offline evidence bundle protocol (see related page: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/offline-artifact-delivery-and-model-refresh-runbook-2026).

Appendix A — Common commands and snippets:
- Verify operator identity:
  redwood-agent whoami
- Export control plane config for replay environment:
  redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --out config-replay.yaml
- Start telemetry capture:
  redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id> --full
- Start replay controller:
  redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>
- Bundle artifacts for evidence:
  tar czf replay-<run-id>-evidence.tgz telemetry-<run-id>.tgz profiler-<run-id>.json metrics-<run-id>.prom

Appendix B — KV cache warm-up script (example):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Warm up top 100 frequently used KV keys for tenant
TENANT=$1
for key in $(cat top-100-keys.txt); do
  curl -sS -X POST "https://local-runtime/kv/warmup" -H "x-tenant: $TENANT" -d "{"key": "$key"}" >/dev/null
done
```

Change log:
- 2026-03-15: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-05-02: Expanded acceptance gates and added KV warm-up script; reviewers: Diego Morales, Liam O'Connor

Related pages and references:
- Post-installation telemetry and acceptance checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Live traffic simulation and SLA validation guidance: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/live-traffic-simulation-and-sla-validation-playbook-2026

Contact for questions: field-ops@redwood.com

text:
Overview:

This playbook describes the standardized process for field operators and on-call engineers to perform offline debugging of private-deploy runtime incidents and to run deterministic synthetic SLA replay tests. Use this when a customer site experiences intermittent or reproducible SLA regressions but live traffic replay is not possible or desirable (air-gapped sites, PII constraints, or when reproducing production load would risk further degradation).

Goals:
- Produce a deterministic reproduction of the failing condition with minimal customer impact.
- Validate mitigation and rollout changes by running synthetic SLA replays against a controlled replica of the tenant environment.
- Capture forensics and telemetry artifacts required for post-incident review and compliance evidence.

When to use this playbook:
- Repeated elevated p95/p99 latency for inference endpoints with no clear live-trigger-able pattern.
- Tenant-facing degradation that cannot be safely reproduced with live traffic (regulated customers, air-gapped installs).
- After a model swap, quantization change, or runtime upgrade that correlates with latency or error regression.

Preconditions (must be satisfied before starting):
1) Operator prerequisites:
   - Operator role: tenant-operator or field-operator (RBAC token present).
   - Local toolkit: redwood-agent v2.4+ installed and authenticated (run redwood-agent whoami).
2) Telemetry and snapshot prerequisites:
   - Telemetry sampling level must include full request/response traces for target tenant (eg: sampling=0.1 or full for timestamp window).
   - A recent state snapshot of model artifacts, KV cache metadata, and runtime config exported to the safe bucket (see Snapshot naming convention below).
3) Safety and approvals:
   - For customer-facing private deployments, secure approval from the customer point-of-contact (email or ticket) is required unless the tenant is in a pre-agreed emergency escalation policy.

Key artifacts to collect before offline replay:
- runtime-metrics-export-<tenant>-<ts>.tgz (CPU/GPU utilization, host-level metrics)
- kv-cache-metadata-<tenant>-<ts>.json (KV cache size, hit/miss rates)
- model-artifact-manifest-<tenant>-<version>.yaml (model checksum, quantization profile)
- request-sample-set-<tenant>-<window>.jsonl (sanitized inputs for replay)

Snapshot naming convention (example):
- <tenant>-snapshot-2026-03-14T14-12-00Z

Environment setup (operator steps):
1) Provision an isolated replay environment (operator sandbox) that mirrors the tenant runtime:
   - Create an ephemeral namespace: redwood-<tenant>-replay-<run-id>
   - Clone control-plane config: redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --to ./config.yaml
   - Ensure model artifacts are mounted read-only from the snapshot bundle.
2) Configure telemetry to capture a full replay trace:
   - redwood-telemetry set sampling --tenant <tenant> --level full --target redis

Synthetic SLA replay specification (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Purpose |
|---|---:|---:|
| duration | 15m | Validate sustained behavior over steady-state window |
| rps | 50 | Start at low RPS then scale (see ramp schedule) |
| concurrency | 8 | Parallel client threads to stress batching paths |
| payload-distribution | 70% short (<=64 tokens), 30% long (>=512 tokens) | Matches observed production token distribution |
| seed | fixed per run-id | Ensure deterministic model sampling when applicable |

Example synthetic workload YAML (paste into replay controller):
```yaml
run_id: replay-2026-05-02-opsA
tenant: example-corp
duration: 900 # seconds
ramp_schedule:
  - { duration: 120, start_rps: 5, end_rps: 20 }
  - { duration: 300, start_rps: 20, end_rps: 50 }
concurrency: 8
payloads: ./request-sample-set-example.jsonl
token_distribution:
  short: 0.7
  long: 0.3
metrics_export: ./replay-metrics/
```

Replay runbook (step-by-step):
1) Validate preconditions and collect pre-run artifacts (see above).
2) Start telemetry capture: redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id>
3) Boot ephemeral replay environment (operator sandbox). Confirm host and GPU topology match production tenant profile (use redwoodctl topology --tenant <tenant>).
4) Load sanitized request-sample-set and configure the replay controller with the run YAML.
5) Start the run in dry-run mode (no model execution) to verify request timing and sampling: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --dry-run.
6) When dry-run succeeds, start an instrumented run: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>.
7) Monitor SLOs live: dashboard link: https://console.internal/redwood/replay/<run-id> (CPU/GPU, queue-latency, KV hit-rate, per-model p95/p99).
8) If observed p95/p99 breaches configured acceptance gates (see Safety gates below), abort and capture full artifacts.
9) If run is stable, iterate: adjust model quantization profile or batching window and re-run to validate improvements.

Acceptance gates and abort criteria (safety):
- Abort immediately if:
  - Error rate increases > 5x baseline and absolute error rate > 1%
  - GPU OOM observed on >1 node during run
  - Host-level thermal emergency or fan-throttle event
- Soft gate: flag run if p99 latency increases by >25% vs baseline for more than 2 consecutive sampling windows (1 minute each).

Post-run forensic collection checklist (automated + manual):
1) redwood-telemetry stop --label replay-<run-id> && redwood-telemetry bundle --label replay-<run-id> --out ./telemetry-<run-id>.tgz
2) Export replay metrics: redwood-replay export-metrics --label replay-<run-id> --format prometheus --out ./metrics-<run-id>.prom
3) Capture model runtime profiler snapshot: redwood-profiler snapshot --label replay-<run-id> --out profiler-<run-id>.json
4) Package all artifacts and upload to secure evidence bucket with run metadata: s3://evidence.redwood/replays/<tenant>/<run-id>/

Rollback and mitigation recommendations (if replay confirms regression):
- Short-term (immediate):
  1) Revert model variant to last-known-good model-artifact-manifest (model swap rollback)
  2) Reduce batching window to force lower queueing latency (config: runtime.batch.window_ms -> 0-5ms)
  3) Throttle tenant RPS via edge-router for controlled traffic shed (policy: tenant-traffic-quota)
- Mid-term:
  1) Apply targeted KV cache warm-up script prior to traffic ramp (see Appendix)
  2) Roll forward a quantization fallback variant (configured in runtime catalog with label quant-fallback)

Owner and escalation matrix:
- Primary: Field Operations (field-ops@redwood.com)
- Secondary: Serving Runtime Team (runtime@redwood.com)
- Escalation to Engineering Manager (on-call): esc-runtime@redwood.com if no mitigation within 2 hours.

SLOs and measurement window for replay validation:
- p95 inference latency target: <= 1200ms for long requests; <= 120ms for short requests
- p99 inference latency target: <= 2500ms for long requests; <= 350ms for short requests
- KV cache hit-rate: >= 85% during steady state (otherwise investigate eviction policy)

Operational notes and best practices:
- Always use sanitized request samples for regulated tenants. Strip any PII and validate with the customer data owner before replay.
- Use the same model seed and deterministic sampling options in the runtime to make replays repeatable. Set RNG seed in config: runtime.model.seed: <run-seed>.
- For air-gapped installs, use the offline evidence bundle protocol (see related page: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/offline-artifact-delivery-and-model-refresh-runbook-2026).

Appendix A — Common commands and snippets:
- Verify operator identity:
  redwood-agent whoami
- Export control plane config for replay environment:
  redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --out config-replay.yaml
- Start telemetry capture:
  redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id> --full
- Start replay controller:
  redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>
- Bundle artifacts for evidence:
  tar czf replay-<run-id>-evidence.tgz telemetry-<run-id>.tgz profiler-<run-id>.json metrics-<run-id>.prom

Appendix B — KV cache warm-up script (example):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Warm up top 100 frequently used KV keys for tenant
TENANT=$1
for key in $(cat top-100-keys.txt); do
  curl -sS -X POST "https://local-runtime/kv/warmup" -H "x-tenant: $TENANT" -d "{\"key\": \"$key\"}" >/dev/null
done
```

Change log:
- 2026-03-15: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-05-02: Expanded acceptance gates and added KV warm-up script; reviewers: Diego Morales, Liam O'Connor

Related pages and references:
- Post-installation telemetry and acceptance checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Live traffic simulation and SLA validation guidance: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/live-traffic-simulation-and-sla-validation-playbook-2026

Contact for questions: field-ops@redwood.com

source.title: staged-quantized-model-rollout-and-forensic-telemetry-playbook
source.author: Maya Lopez
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook describes a conservative, operator-friendly process for rolling out quantized model variants in private (VPC / on-prem) deployments while capturing forensic telemetry sufficient for root cause analysis and potential regulatory evidence preservation. It covers staged rollout patterns, preflight checks, metric/trace/event contracts, alerting thresholds, and a tested rollback path that preserves artifact provenance and degraded-model evidence.

Scope:
- Applies to model binary changes that modify numeric representation (int8/4/bsr/FP16) and associated runtime flags (KV cache format, kernel selection, weight packing).
- Target platforms: Redwood Private (VPC) and on-prem packaged runtimes managed by operator teams.
- Not in scope: serving-layer protocol changes, control-plane schema migrations (separate upgrade playbook).

Goals and success criteria:
1) No more than 1.5x increase in tail latency (P99) vs baseline for quantized model serving under canonical workload.
2) No more than 0.5% degradation in quality signatures for evaluated prompt-sets during rollout window (see Quality Signals section).
3) Maintain forensic telemetry snapshot for any failed rollout for at least 90 days or customer retention window.
4) Zero-customer-data leakage during artifact staging and rollback.

Owner and escalation:
- Primary: Model Operations (modelops@redwood.com), on-call: modelops-rotations (pager duty rule: ModelOps_Push).
- Secondary: Runtime Reliability Team (eng-serving-runtime).
- Escalation matrix: operator -> modelops -> runtime -> security (if evidence indicates data exposure).

Prerequisites and preflight checklist:
- Artifact provenance: model build signed and attestation file present (SHA256 + signature).
- Customer contract: private deploy must have approved quantization policy (customer may opt out of certain quant levels).
- Canary environment: isolated tenant with synthetic workload and traffic shadowing enabled.
- Telemetry pipeline: forensic-event topic writable, 14d rolling retention for detailed spans, 90d storage for compressed forensic bundles.
- Backups: KV-cache snapshot and tenant state snapshot (t-0) stored in immutable archive.

Preflight command snippets (examples):
- Verify artifact signature: 
  openssl dgst -sha256 -verify pubkey.pem -signature quantized_model.sig quantized_model.bin
- Generate KV-cache snapshot: 
  redwoodctl tenant snapshot --tenant-id=tenant-abc --output=/var/backups/tenant-abc-kv-$(date +%F).tar.gz
- Validate runtime compatibility: 
  redwood-runtime-check --image quantized_model.bin --seq-length=1024 --kernels=autoselect

Rollout strategy (stages):
1) Local operator validation (single-node): smoke test, perf harness at 10qps for 10m.
2) Isolated canary (internal tenant): 0.5% of production traffic, shadow real requests, no direct customer routing. Run 4h minimum and pass quality/latency gates.
3) Incremental production ramp (slope-based): add 1% production traffic every 30 minutes with monitoring gating.
4) Burst window testing: enable 10x synthetic bursts at 2x expected QPS to validate evictions and thermal behavior.
5) Full cutover only after 24h at >=10% traffic with metrics green.

Gating criteria (automated):
- Latency: P50/P95 remain within 1.2x of baseline; P99 <= 1.5x baseline. (Metric: runtime.infer.latency.{p50,p95,p99})
- Error rate: 5xx rate < 0.1% and stable. (Metric: runtime.request.errors)
- Quality: Prompt-set drift score < 0.5% relative to baseline (see Quality Signals).
- Resource pressure: GPU memory pressure < 85%, thermal throttle events = 0.
- Telemetry completeness: forensic-event publish success rate >= 99.9%.

Quality Signals (examples):
- Per-prompt token perplexity delta and embedding cosine drift on a canonical prompt-set.
- Safety filter hit-rate change vs baseline.
- Deterministic regression checks for templated prompts (yes/no matches).

Forensic telemetry contract (what to capture):
- Trace/span context for every canary inference request including: request_id, tenant_id (hashed), model_variant_id, kernel_path, quant_profile, runtime_flags, shard_node_id, timestamp, sequence_length, kv_cache_hit_ratio.
- Compact artifacts: a 30-minute compressed forensic bundle on failure including: request traces, a 1% sample of request/response pairs (redacted PII), runtime logs from affected nodes, GPU telemetry (power/thermal), kernel failure dumps.
- Evidence tagging: all forensic bundles include a short provenance.json with builder, git-commit, build-timestamp, signature, and operator ID.

Alerting (examples):
- Alert A1: P99 latency spike > 1.5x baseline sustained for 5 minutes -> SEV2 to runtime oncall.
- Alert A2: Quality drift > 0.5% on canonical prompt-set measured in canary -> SEV2 to modelops and product.
- Alert A3: Forensic-event publish failure > 0.1% for 10m -> SEV1 (possible telemetry outage).

Rollforward automation and safe stops:
- The orchestrator exposes staged-flags: model.variant.weight (0-100), model.variant.burst-allow, model.variant-kernel-blacklist.
- A safe-stop webhook halts all percentage ramps and reverts traffic to previous variant if any gating metric trips.
- Recommended operator action on safe-stop: capture forensic bundle, mark rollout as halted in the rollout ledger, notify customers if SLA-affecting.

Step-by-step example (operator runbook):
1) Prepare artifacts and run preflight commands. Confirm signature and snapshot.
2) Upload quantized artifact to staging registry and set model.metadata.quant_profile = "int8-channelwise-v1".
3) Start a local smoke on a single isolated node: redwoodctl run-smoke --model=quantized:staging --qps=10 --duration=10m.
4) If smoke passes, create canary rollout: redwoodctl start-rollout --model=quantized:staging --target=canary-tenant --initial-weight=0.5
5) Enable forensic capture for canary: redwoodctl telemetry enable --scope=canary-tenant --forensic=true
6) Monitor gating dashboards (Latency / Quality / Telemetry completeness) for 4 hours.
7) If green, ramp to production in controlled steps: for weight in 1 2 5 10 25; do redwoodctl set-rollout-weight --id=<rollout-id> --weight=$weight; wait 30m and validate gates.
8) After hitting 10% and observing 24h stability, complete cutover: redwoodctl finalize-rollout --id=<rollout-id>.

Rollback and artifact sanitization steps (on failure):
1) Trigger abort: redwoodctl abort-rollout --id=<rollout-id> --reason="gating-failure"
2) Immediately set traffic back to previous variant: redwoodctl set-rollout-weight --id=<rollout-id> --weight=0 --target=previous-variant
3) Preserve forensic bundle: redwoodctl forensic export --rollout-id=<rollout-id> --output=/evidence/<rollout-id>.tar.gz
4) Run artifact sanitization (ensure no customer PII left in sample): redwoodctl evidence redact --input=/evidence/<rollout-id>.tar.gz --policy=pii-strict
5) Document incident in the rollout ledger: redwoodctl rollout-note --id=<rollout-id> --note="Rollback due to P99 latency" --owner=$USER
6) Open postmortem and schedule a gated reattempt only after root cause triage and approval.

Data retention and compliance notes:
- Forensic bundles retained 90 days by default; regulated customers may require extended retention — consult legal.
- Access to forensic bundles is RBAC-protected. Any copy outside the platform requires manager-level approval and a signed access justification.
- Immutable artifacts (signed binaries, provenance.json) stored in immutable object store with WORM enabled for the customer retention window.

Testing checklist (operator acceptance tests):
- Functional: templated deterministic requests produce expected tokens for 95% of templates.
- Latency: baseline synthetic p99 within 1.5x across three independent runs.
- Safety: safety classifier false-positive rate within expected bounds.
- Telemetry: forensic bundle contains at least 95% of expected trace/span fields.

Appendix A — Example rollout metadata (JSON snippet):
{
  "rollout_id": "rollout-2026-03-04-quant1",
  "model": "mosaic-qlora-7b",
  "variant": "int8-channelwise-v1",
  "initiated_by": "maya.lopez@redwood.com",
  "start_ts": "2026-03-04T15:20:00Z"
}

Appendix B — Recommended observability query examples:
- P99 latency for model variant: sum(rate(runtime_infer_latency_bucket{le=\"1.0\",model_variant=~\"int8.*\"}[5m])) by (model_variant)
- Quality drift: custom_eval_drift{prompt_set=\"canonical-2026\",model_variant=\"int8-channelwise-v1\"}

Appendix C — Common troubleshooting playbook (short):
- Symptom: sudden P99 spike with no errors.
  - Check GPU memory pressure and kernel recompile logs on nodes in the rollout shard.
  - If kernel OOMs are present, blacklist offending kernel and reattempt with fallback kernel_path.
- Symptom: rising forensic-event publish errors.
  - Validate telemetry broker health, check disk space on telemetry proxy, restart agent with redwoodctl telemetry restart-agent.
- Symptom: deterministic regression on templated prompts.
  - Capture offending request/response pair from forensic bundle, compare float vs quantized decoding, escalate to modelops for re-quantization or bias-correction.

Related resources and links:
- Model artifact supply chain spec (onprem packaging): /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/model-artifact-supplychain-and-image-attestation-spec-2026
- KV-cache migration playbook: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/kv-cache-migrations-and-runtime-state-migration-playbook-2025

Change history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft by Maya Lopez (initial runbook).
- 2026-02-10: Added telemetry contract and forensic bundle format (review by Ethan Park).
- 2026-03-04: Operationalized gating thresholds and finalized rollback sanitization steps (published).

End of document.
