- Every alert includes:
  - A short summary
  - Severity (`sev1`, `sev2`, `sev3`)
  - A “what to do first” hint
  - A runbook link (internal by default; externalized in customer-facing docs)

## Air-gapped upgrade & rollback guidance
### Golden rule
Upgrade the observability bundle in lockstep with Redwood Private **minor** releases unless release notes explicitly declare compatibility.

### Supported upgrade paths
- Supported: `1.18.x` -> `1.19.x` (standard minor upgrade)
- Supported: patch upgrades within the same minor (`1.18.0` -> `1.18.3`)
- Not supported without review: skipping more than one minor version (e.g., `1.17` -> `1.19`)

### What can change across versions
- New or renamed metrics (dashboards and rules will be updated accordingly).
- Alert threshold tuning and enable/disable defaults.
- Dashboard panel queries and variable naming.
- Upstream image pins (Prometheus/Grafana/Loki).

### Backward compatibility commitments
We aim to preserve:
- Stable metric names for top-level golden signals.
- Stable dashboard UIDs within a major release line.

When breaking changes occur:
- They are called out in the Redwood Private release notes.
- A migration note is included in the bundle manifest metadata.

### Rollback considerations
If you rollback Redwood Private:
- Also rollback dashboards/rules to the previous bundle version.
- Keep Prometheus TSDB data; do not delete PVCs unless instructed.
- If alert noise spikes after rollback, temporarily disable the `SLO burn` group until rules are aligned.

## Storage and sizing defaults (baseline)
These defaults are chosen to be safe for typical Private footprints and are validated in reference environments.

Prometheus:
- Retention: 15 days
- Storage: 200Gi (PVC), expandable
- CPU/memory: 2 vCPU / 8Gi baseline (scale up with scrape volume)

Grafana:
- Storage: optional (PVC) if persisting dashboards/users; otherwise ephemeral is acceptable for bundled dashboards.

Logging collector:
- Buffering defaults are tuned to avoid unbounded memory usage.
- If the customer requires “no log loss,” enable disk-backed buffering and ensure adequate node storage.

For detailed sizing by request volume, see the Infra runbook for Prometheus/Grafana sizing.

## Common customer constraints and recommended responses
1) “We need all artifacts as a single signed tarball”
- Supported. Redwood ships a single compressed bundle plus checksum and signature metadata.

2) “We cannot run Grafana in-cluster; must use central Grafana”
- Supported in Mode A. Provide dashboards as JSON plus provisioning guidance.

3) “We must forward logs to Splunk/ELK; no Loki allowed”
- Supported. Use the `logging/pipelines/` templates and configure collector output accordingly.

4) “No outbound DNS / egress”
- Supported. Ensure charts do not reference external image registries; set `global.imageRegistry` and mirror all digests.

## Validation checklist (air-gapped)
Before handing off to customers / declaring readiness:
- [ ] `sha256sum -c` passes for all files in `manifest/SHA256SUMS`.
- [ ] Bundle manifest signature verified (connected environment) OR offline verification packet delivered.
- [ ] All included images mirrored; digests match manifest.
- [ ] Installer preflight checks pass (storage class, PVC provisioning, NetworkPolicy reachability).
- [ ] Prometheus scrapes Redwood targets (no `up == 0` for Redwood jobs > 5m).
- [ ] Grafana dashboards render and show non-empty panels after generating sample traffic.
- [ ] At least one alert can be triggered in staging via failure injection (scrape failure or simulated 5xx).

## Troubleshooting pointers
- Dashboards empty: confirm datasource name and Prometheus job labels; check scrape permissions (NetworkPolicies).
- Prometheus OOM / TSDB churn: reduce scrape interval, increase resources, and verify cardinality constraints; confirm no high-cardinality labels were enabled.
- Log pipeline backpressure: increase buffers, reduce batch size, confirm sink auth (Splunk HEC token / ELK credentials).

See: Private observability Day-2 runbook for step-by-step triage.

## Ownership and release process
Owners:
- Packaging/release: Connor O'Brien (Private Deployments), Chloe Martin (Grafana packaging)
- Observability standards: Allison Grant
- Telemetry platform: Logan Wright
- Security review: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

Release process (summary):
1) Cut release branch for Redwood Private.
2) Build observability bundle from pinned digests + approved dashboards/rules.
3) Generate `bundle-manifest.json`, SBOM, and checksums.
4) Sign manifest; publish bundle to the customer delivery channel.
5) Validate in reference air-gapped environment (see validation plan).

## Changelog
- 2025-09-03: Updated default tooling compatibility (Grafana 10.4+), clarified signature verification paths, and expanded mirroring examples.
- 2025-08-12: Added explicit Mode A vs Mode B guidance and tightened digest enforcement language.
- 2025-07-18: Initial published version for Private minimum pack offline delivery.
text:
## Overview
This page defines how Redwood packages and delivers the **Private observability minimum pack** for **offline / air-gapped on-prem** environments.

Goals:
- Ensure customers can install a supported baseline observability setup without outbound internet.
- Provide a deterministic, verifiable artifact bundle (images + dashboards + rules + configs) that can be mirrored into a customer registry.
- Make upgrades/rollbacks safe: versions are pinned, manifests are checksummed/signed, and compatibility constraints are documented.

Non-goals (v1):
- Customer-specific dashboard customization in the bundle.
- Long-retention or federation “premium” observability features.

## Audience
- Customer platform / infra teams operating Redwood Private in on-prem or restricted networks.
- Redwood Solutions Engineering and Support assisting air-gapped installs.
- Redwood Private Deployments engineers maintaining the offline packaging.

## Support policy (what we support)
We support:
- The official offline bundle produced by Redwood release tooling for a specific Redwood Private release.
- Mirroring into a customer-managed registry (Harbor/Artifactory/ECR-on-VM/etc.) using standard tools (skopeo/crane/docker).
- Helm-based installation via `redwood-private-installer` and `redwood-helm-charts` with offline overrides.

We do not support:
- Customer-built images or repackaged dashboards/rules not matching the bundle manifest (unless explicitly approved in a support engagement).

## Bundle versioning
The offline observability bundle is versioned alongside Redwood Private.

Naming convention:
- Bundle filename: `redwood-private-observability-offline-bundle-<private_version>-<bundle_build>.tar.zst`
- Example: `redwood-private-observability-offline-bundle-1.18.0-b3.tar.zst`

The bundle is considered compatible only with the matching Redwood Private version (see “Compatibility & upgrades”).

## Bundle contents
The offline bundle contains **three classes** of artifacts:

1) Container images (OCI)
- Required images for the minimum-pack observability components.
- Redwood-owned images (where applicable) plus pinned upstream images.

2) Grafana dashboards
- JSON dashboards in a versioned directory.
- Provisioning metadata for folder placement and datasources.

3) Prometheus rules
- Alerting rules and recording rules shipped as YAML.
- Rules include runbook links and are aligned to the Private alert severity taxonomy.

### Directory layout inside the bundle
Top-level structure (logical):
- `manifest/`
  - `bundle-manifest.json` (list of artifacts and digests)
  - `bundle-sbom.spdx.json` (SBOM for images + key files)
  - `SHA256SUMS`
  - `cosign.bundle.sig` (signature payload; see “Signing & verification”)
- `images/`
  - `index.json` (mapping of source image -> digest -> target tag suggestion)
  - `oci/` (OCI image layout) OR `docker-archive/` (depending on release tooling)
- `grafana/`
  - `dashboards/private-min-pack/<version>/*.json`
  - `provisioning/` (folders + dashboard providers)
- `prometheus/`
  - `rules/alerts/*.yaml`
  - `rules/recording/*.yaml`
- `logging/`
  - `collector/` (default config snippets)
  - `pipelines/` (Loki/ELK/Splunk examples; templates)

Note: Exact layout may evolve, but `manifest/bundle-manifest.json` is the source of truth for what the bundle contains.

## Container images to mirror (minimum pack)
The minimum pack is designed to work with customer-run Prometheus/Grafana, but our offline bundle ships a supported baseline “batteries included” stack for on-prem installs when customers do not already have standard tooling.

### Image set (baseline)
The following images are included and must be mirrored into the customer registry if deploying the included components:

Core observability:
- `quay.io/prometheus/prometheus:<pinned>`
- `quay.io/prometheus/alertmanager:<pinned>`
- `quay.io/prometheus/node-exporter:<pinned>`
- `quay.io/prometheus-operator/prometheus-operator:<pinned>` (if using the operator path)
- `grafana/grafana:<pinned>`

Kubernetes metrics:
- `registry.k8s.io/metrics-server/metrics-server:<pinned>` (only if enabled by values)
- `registry.k8s.io/kube-state-metrics/kube-state-metrics:<pinned>`

Logging (default collector; see ADR-022):
- `grafana/loki:<pinned>` (optional; enabled when using the bundled Loki path)
- `grafana/promtail:<pinned>` OR `grafana/alloy:<pinned>` (depending on bundle revision)
- `otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:<pinned>` (optional; only if OTLP export is enabled)

Redwood components (for dashboards/rules parity):
- No additional Redwood application images are introduced by the observability bundle; it only references metrics/logs emitted by existing Redwood Private components.

### Image set notes
- All images are pinned by **digest** in `manifest/bundle-manifest.json`. Tags are advisory.
- If your environment forbids external registries, mirror all images to a single internal registry namespace (recommended).
- If you already operate Prometheus/Grafana, you may disable bundled deployment and use only dashboards/rules. In that case, you still need to ensure your existing versions meet the compatibility matrix below.

## Compatibility matrix (minimum supported versions)
These are the minimum supported upstream versions for air-gapped installs using the minimum pack artifacts.

- Kubernetes: 1.26–1.30
- Prometheus: >= 2.45 (recommended 2.50+)
- Alertmanager: >= 0.26
- Grafana: >= 10.4
- Loki (if enabled): >= 2.9

If customers run older versions, dashboards may fail to render, PromQL may error, or alert expressions may be invalid. Support may ask you to upgrade tooling before triaging Redwood-specific issues.

## Signing & verification
Air-gapped installs require customers to validate artifact integrity.

### Checksums
The bundle includes `manifest/SHA256SUMS` covering:
- The compressed bundle file (when distributed as a single archive)
- `bundle-manifest.json`
- All dashboard JSON files
- All Prometheus rule YAML files

Customer verification (example):
1. Extract bundle: `tar --use-compress-program=unzstd -xvf redwood-private-observability-offline-bundle-<ver>.tar.zst`
2. Verify checksums:
   - `cd manifest && sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS`

### Signatures
Redwood signs the bundle manifest using Sigstore/cosign.

Verification steps (connected environment, recommended before moving into the air-gap):
- `cosign verify-blob \
    --certificate-identity "redwood-releases@redwoodinference.com" \
    --certificate-oidc-issuer "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
    --signature manifest/cosign.bundle.sig \
    manifest/bundle-manifest.json`

Air-gapped verification:
- Redwood can provide an offline verification packet (certificate + Rekor inclusion proof) per release if required by customer policy. Coordinate via Solutions Engineering.

### Image digest enforcement
When mirroring, ensure the final registry references match the digests in the manifest.
- Acceptable: retagging while preserving digest.
- Not acceptable: rebuilding images or mirroring via a process that changes layers.

## Mirroring images into a customer registry
Customers should mirror images from the bundle into an internal registry accessible by the cluster.

### Recommended tools
- `skopeo` (preferred for air-gapped mirroring)
- `crane` (OK)
- `docker load` + `docker push` (works, slower; avoid for very large bundles)

### Example: mirror using skopeo (OCI layout)
Assumptions:
- Internal registry: `registry.customer.local/redwood-observability`
- Bundle extracted to `/bundles/obs/`

Procedure:
1) Authenticate to internal registry:
- `skopeo login registry.customer.local`

2) Iterate images from `images/index.json` (scripted by customer; Redwood installer also supports a mirror command in some environments):
- For each entry, copy digest-pinned image:
  - `skopeo copy --all \
      oci:/bundles/obs/images/oci/<image_path>@sha256:<digest> \
      docker://registry.customer.local/redwood-observability/<name>:<tag>`

3) Validate digests:
- `skopeo inspect docker://registry.customer.local/redwood-observability/<name>:<tag> | jq .Digest`
- Compare to `bundle-manifest.json`.

### Example: mirror using docker-archive
If the bundle ships `docker-archive/` tarballs:
- `docker load -i images/docker-archive/prometheus.tar`
- `docker tag <loaded_image_id> registry.customer.local/redwood-observability/prometheus:<tag>`
- `docker push registry.customer.local/redwood-observability/prometheus:<tag>`

## Helm / installer configuration for air-gapped installs
There are two supported modes:

### Mode A: Use customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana
Use this when the customer already has a standard observability stack.

Required actions:
- Install Redwood dashboards and Prometheus rules from the bundle into the customer stack.
- Configure datasource names and scrape configs to match Redwood expectations.

Key values (examples; exact keys may vary by chart version):
- `observability.enabled=true`
- `observability.prometheus.deploy=false`
- `observability.grafana.deploy=false`
- `observability.dashboards.install=true`
- `observability.rules.install=true`

### Mode B: Deploy bundled Prometheus/Grafana (baseline)
Use this when the customer requests a turnkey supported stack.

Required actions:
- Mirror images to internal registry.
- Provide image registry override values.
- Ensure storage classes and PVC sizing meet minimums.

Key values (examples):
- `global.imageRegistry=registry.customer.local`
- `observability.prometheus.deploy=true`
- `observability.grafana.deploy=true`
- `observability.prometheus.retention=15d` (default)
- `observability.prometheus.storage.size=200Gi` (default baseline; see sizing guidance)

Note: If NetworkPolicies are enabled in the cluster, you must allow Prometheus scrapes of Redwood namespaces (see Day-2 runbook).

## Dashboards and rule artifacts
### Grafana dashboards
Location in bundle:
- `grafana/dashboards/private-min-pack/<version>/`

Dashboards included (v1 minimum set):
- Runtime overview (golden signals, token throughput, batching/KV cache, GPU utilization)
- Control plane health (gateway/auth/policy/config services)
- GPU & node resources (GPU saturation, memory pressure, node exporter)
- Queueing/backpressure (request queue depth, throttling, 429s)
- Error analysis (5xx, upstream dependency errors, restart loops)

Installation patterns:
- Grafana provisioning (file-based) for bundled Grafana.
- ConfigMap import (Kubernetes) for customer-managed Grafana, if they accept provisioned dashboards.

Datasource expectations:
- Prometheus datasource named `Prometheus` (default) OR override via values.

### Prometheus rules
Location in bundle:
- `prometheus/rules/alerts/`
- `prometheus/rules/recording/`

Rule design notes:
- Alerts are designed for bounded cardinality in customer Prometheus.
- SLO burn alerts are included where feasible; some are disabled by default to avoid paging noise.
- Every alert includes:
  - A short summary
  - Severity (`sev1`, `sev2`, `sev3`)
  - A “what to do first” hint
  - A runbook link (internal by default; externalized in customer-facing docs)

## Air-gapped upgrade & rollback guidance
### Golden rule
Upgrade the observability bundle in lockstep with Redwood Private **minor** releases unless release notes explicitly declare compatibility.

### Supported upgrade paths
- Supported: `1.18.x` -> `1.19.x` (standard minor upgrade)
- Supported: patch upgrades within the same minor (`1.18.0` -> `1.18.3`)
- Not supported without review: skipping more than one minor version (e.g., `1.17` -> `1.19`)

### What can change across versions
- New or renamed metrics (dashboards and rules will be updated accordingly).
- Alert threshold tuning and enable/disable defaults.
- Dashboard panel queries and variable naming.
- Upstream image pins (Prometheus/Grafana/Loki).

### Backward compatibility commitments
We aim to preserve:
- Stable metric names for top-level golden signals.
- Stable dashboard UIDs within a major release line.

When breaking changes occur:
- They are called out in the Redwood Private release notes.
- A migration note is included in the bundle manifest metadata.

### Rollback considerations
If you rollback Redwood Private:
- Also rollback dashboards/rules to the previous bundle version.
- Keep Prometheus TSDB data; do not delete PVCs unless instructed.
- If alert noise spikes after rollback, temporarily disable the `SLO burn` group until rules are aligned.

## Storage and sizing defaults (baseline)
These defaults are chosen to be safe for typical Private footprints and are validated in reference environments.

Prometheus:
- Retention: 15 days
- Storage: 200Gi (PVC), expandable
- CPU/memory: 2 vCPU / 8Gi baseline (scale up with scrape volume)

Grafana:
- Storage: optional (PVC) if persisting dashboards/users; otherwise ephemeral is acceptable for bundled dashboards.

Logging collector:
- Buffering defaults are tuned to avoid unbounded memory usage.
- If the customer requires “no log loss,” enable disk-backed buffering and ensure adequate node storage.

For detailed sizing by request volume, see the Infra runbook for Prometheus/Grafana sizing.

## Common customer constraints and recommended responses
1) “We need all artifacts as a single signed tarball”
- Supported. Redwood ships a single compressed bundle plus checksum and signature metadata.

2) “We cannot run Grafana in-cluster; must use central Grafana”
- Supported in Mode A. Provide dashboards as JSON plus provisioning guidance.

3) “We must forward logs to Splunk/ELK; no Loki allowed”
- Supported. Use the `logging/pipelines/` templates and configure collector output accordingly.

4) “No outbound DNS / egress”
- Supported. Ensure charts do not reference external image registries; set `global.imageRegistry` and mirror all digests.

## Validation checklist (air-gapped)
Before handing off to customers / declaring readiness:
- [ ] `sha256sum -c` passes for all files in `manifest/SHA256SUMS`.
- [ ] Bundle manifest signature verified (connected environment) OR offline verification packet delivered.
- [ ] All included images mirrored; digests match manifest.
- [ ] Installer preflight checks pass (storage class, PVC provisioning, NetworkPolicy reachability).
- [ ] Prometheus scrapes Redwood targets (no `up == 0` for Redwood jobs > 5m).
- [ ] Grafana dashboards render and show non-empty panels after generating sample traffic.
- [ ] At least one alert can be triggered in staging via failure injection (scrape failure or simulated 5xx).

## Troubleshooting pointers
- Dashboards empty: confirm datasource name and Prometheus job labels; check scrape permissions (NetworkPolicies).
- Prometheus OOM / TSDB churn: reduce scrape interval, increase resources, and verify cardinality constraints; confirm no high-cardinality labels were enabled.
- Log pipeline backpressure: increase buffers, reduce batch size, confirm sink auth (Splunk HEC token / ELK credentials).

See: Private observability Day-2 runbook for step-by-step triage.

## Ownership and release process
Owners:
- Packaging/release: Connor O'Brien (Private Deployments), Chloe Martin (Grafana packaging)
- Observability standards: Allison Grant
- Telemetry platform: Logan Wright
- Security review: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

Release process (summary):
1) Cut release branch for Redwood Private.
2) Build observability bundle from pinned digests + approved dashboards/rules.
3) Generate `bundle-manifest.json`, SBOM, and checksums.
4) Sign manifest; publish bundle to the customer delivery channel.
5) Validate in reference air-gapped environment (see validation plan).

## Changelog
- 2025-09-03: Updated default tooling compatibility (Grafana 10.4+), clarified signature verification paths, and expanded mirroring examples.
- 2025-08-12: Added explicit Mode A vs Mode B guidance and tightened digest enforcement language.
- 2025-07-18: Initial published version for Private minimum pack offline delivery.

source.title: Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1) — Overview
source.author: Elliot Price
source.content:
## Purpose
The **Private Observability Minimum Pack** is the baseline, supported observability bundle shipped with **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem) installs. It provides a standard set of:
- **Grafana dashboards** (health and performance for control plane + data plane)
- **Prometheus alert rules + recording rules** (golden signals + resource pressure)
- **Structured log collection defaults + export integrations** (sane defaults that work in private environments)

This pack is designed so that:
- Customer platform teams can monitor and operate Redwood Private day-2 without bespoke setup.
- Redwood Support/SRE can troubleshoot efficiently using a consistent set of signals.

## What this pack is (and is not)
### In scope (v1)
- Metrics for: control plane services, API gateway, policy/routing, serving runtime pods, installer/upgrade controller, and key integration points (e.g., model registry).
- A curated set of dashboards and alerting that covers the highest-impact failure modes:
  - Availability, latency, error rate
  - Saturation (GPU/CPU/memory), queueing/backpressure
  - Restart loops / crash loops
  - Storage pressure (Prometheus TSDB PVC) and scrape health
- Log pipeline defaults for control plane and runtime logs with redaction-safe guidance.
- Packaging for **online (VPC)** and **offline/air-gapped (on-prem)** installs.

### Out of scope (v1)
- “Premium” observability: long retention defaults, multi-cluster federation, highly customized dashboards, full distributed tracing with customer-specific sampling policies.
- A Redwood-managed SIEM. Instead, we provide supported export paths to common sinks.

## Supported environments
### Deployment modes
- **Redwood Private (VPC)**: supported
- **Redwood Private (on-prem / air-gapped)**: supported (via offline bundle)

### Kubernetes and distro assumptions
The pack is validated on reference environments used by Private deployments. As a baseline, Redwood Support expects:
- A supported Kubernetes version per the current Private release notes
- A functional cluster DNS and CNI
- Ability to create PVCs (for Prometheus/Grafana as configured)

If you operate OpenShift or other distros, the pack generally works, but any required SecurityContext/PSA constraints may require customer-side adaptation.

## What’s included
### 1) Grafana dashboards (minimum supported set)
The dashboards ship as versioned JSON and are provisioned automatically when the pack is enabled.

**Dashboard set (v1):**
| Dashboard | Primary audience | What it answers |
|---|---|---|
| Private Runtime Overview | Platform / Oncall | “Are requests succeeding? What’s p95/p99 latency? How many tokens/sec? Is batching helping? Any queue buildup?” |
| Control Plane Health | Platform / Support | “Are core services healthy (gateway, auth integration, policy engine)? Any elevated 5xx or dependency failures?” |
| GPU & Node Resources | Platform / SRE | “Are GPUs saturated? Any GPU memory pressure/OOM? Node CPU/mem pressure?” |
| Prometheus / Scrape Health | Platform | “Are targets being scraped? Any stale series? Is TSDB unhealthy or out of space?” |
| Logging Pipeline Health | Platform | “Is the log collector falling behind? Dropping? Retry/backpressure?” |

Notes:
- Dashboards are designed to be **low-cardinality safe**. We avoid per-request labels and limit high-cardinality breakdowns (e.g., model/route) to aggregated or top-N views.
- Dashboards include “**What to check first**” panels for common incidents.

### 2) Prometheus alert rules + recording rules
Alerts are installed as PrometheusRule resources (when enabled). Recording rules provide stable time series for dashboards and for alert evaluation.

**Alert philosophy**
- Alerts align to Private SLO guidance and are tuned for private cluster realities (varying traffic, customer network policies, constrained resources).
- Severity is standardized:
  - **Sev1 (page)**: likely customer-visible outage or rapid SLO burn
  - **Sev2 (page or urgent ticket)**: high risk of degradation/outage if unaddressed
  - **Sev3 (ticket)**: operational issue, early warning, or configuration drift

**Minimum alert groups (v1):**
| Group | Examples | Default severity |
|---|---|---|
| Availability & Error Rate | API 5xx elevated, gateway upstream failures | Sev1/Sev2 |
| Latency & SLO Burn | p95/p99 latency burn rate, request timeouts | Sev1/Sev2 |
| GPU Saturation & OOM | GPU utilization pinned, GPU memory near limit, OOMKilled inference pods | Sev2 |
| Queueing / Backpressure | queue depth growing, batcher backlog sustained | Sev2/Sev3 |
| Restart Loops | CrashLoopBackOff on critical services | Sev2 |
| Scrape / Telemetry Health | scrape target down, rules failing to evaluate, Prometheus TSDB issues | Sev2/Sev3 |
| Storage Pressure | Prometheus PVC filling quickly, disk space low | Sev2 |

Runbook linkage:
- Each alert includes annotations with:
  - Symptom summary
  - “What to do first” steps
  - Link to the Private day-2 runbook section

### 3) Logs: collection defaults + export options
The minimum pack standardizes **structured logs** and offers supported export patterns suitable for private environments.

**Log sources (v1):**
- Control plane services (API gateway, auth/RBAC integration points, policy/routing service)
- Serving runtime pods
- Installer/upgrade controller

**Structured fields (typical):**
- `timestamp`, `level`, `service`, `component`, `namespace`, `pod`, `node`
- `request_id` (generated at gateway)
- `route` (bounded / normalized)
- `model` (bounded / normalized)
- `tenant_id_hash` (when applicable; hashed)
- `status_code`, `latency_ms`, `tokens_in`, `tokens_out` (where applicable)

**Redaction and safety**
- The minimum pack is designed to avoid secrets/PII in logs by default.
- Do not enable request/response payload logging in production unless explicitly approved by your security team.
- See: “Private observability and audit log interactions” for retention and redaction guidance.

**Supported export targets (documented patterns):**
- **Loki** (Grafana stack)
- **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
- **Splunk HEC**
- **OTLP-compatible endpoint** (where feasible; typically customer-managed collector)

## Installation and enablement
The minimum pack is shipped as a versioned artifact and wired into both:
- **`redwood-private-installer`** (recommended for most customers)
- **`redwood-helm-charts`** (for teams operating directly via Helm/GitOps)

### A) Enable via Private Installer (recommended)
1. During install configuration, set:
   - `observability.minimumPack.enabled=true`
2. Choose one of:
   - **Bring your own Prometheus/Grafana** (recommended if you already run them)
   - **Install reference Prometheus/Grafana** via the provided chart options (sizing required)
3. Choose logging mode:
   - Local collection with retention defaults
   - Export to a supported sink (Loki/ELK/Splunk/OTLP)
4. Run installer preflights:
   - Storage class and PVC provisioning
   - NetworkPolicy reachability for scrape targets
   - Resource requests/limits sanity check
5. Post-install validation:
   - Prometheus scrape health
   - Dashboards provisioned and populated
   - A small set of “canary” alerts appear (not necessarily firing)

### B) Enable via Helm (GitOps / advanced)
Enable the pack in your Private values:
- `observabilityPack.enabled: true`
- Configure Grafana provisioning (folder, datasource name)
- Configure PrometheusRule installation behavior
- Configure log collector and sink

Operational note:
- If you already have cluster-wide Prometheus/Grafana, do not install a second copy unless required. Instead, point the pack to your existing stack and ensure scrape configs include Redwood namespaces.

## Versioning strategy
### Pack versioning
- The minimum pack is versioned and released in lockstep with Redwood Private releases.
- The pack follows semantic versioning **within** the Private release stream:
  - **Patch**: dashboard fixes, threshold tuning, non-breaking alert changes
  - **Minor**: additive dashboards/alerts/metrics, new export options, recording rule additions
  - **Major**: breaking changes to metric names, dashboard schema, or required components (avoided; requires explicit migration guidance)

### Compatibility rules
- Metric names and labels are treated as an API for customers.
- We aim for backward compatibility across at least one Private minor release.
- If a breaking metric change is unavoidable:
  - We provide a documented migration
  - We include a temporary compatibility recording rule (where possible)
  - We validate upgrade and rollback paths

## Support boundary (what Redwood Support will ask for)
For tickets involving performance, errors, or instability, Redwood Support may request:
- A screenshot/export of the **Runtime Overview** and **Control Plane Health** dashboards for the incident window
- Prometheus “targets” status for Redwood scrape jobs
- Relevant structured logs filtered by `request_id`/time range
- Confirmation of:
  - Observability pack version
  - Private release version
  - Any custom NetworkPolicies or scrape restrictions

If the minimum pack is disabled or significantly modified, Support may ask you to reproduce the issue with the supported defaults to reduce time-to-resolution.

## Known limitations and operational notes
- High-cardinality breakdowns (per-tenant/per-user) are intentionally not supported in v1 to protect customer Prometheus performance.
- Trace support is limited in v1; use logs + metrics as the primary troubleshooting path.
- Air-gapped installs require that all images and dashboard/rule artifacts come from the offline bundle.

## Quick validation checklist (after install/upgrade)
1. Prometheus targets show **UP** for Redwood endpoints.
2. Grafana folder **“Redwood Private (Minimum Pack)”** exists and dashboards render.
3. The Runtime Overview shows non-zero traffic after a test request.
4. No continuous rule evaluation errors in Prometheus.
5. Log collector shows stable throughput (no sustained drop/backpressure).

## Ownership and escalation
- Product owner: **Vivek Kulkarni** (Private Deployments)
- Engineering owners:
  - Control plane observability: **Ethan Park**
  - Telemetry platform / pack plumbing: **Logan Wright**
  - SRE validation & on-call readiness: **Rafael Mendes**
- Security review: **Marcus Lin**

For day-2 procedures, see the Private Observability Day-2 Runbook.

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1) — Overview
preview:
## Purpose
The **Private Observability Minimum Pack** is the baseline, supported observability bundle shipped with **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem) installs. It provides a standard set of:
- **Grafana dashboards** (health and performance for control plane + data plane)
- **Prometheus alert rules + recording rules** (golden signals + resource pressure)
- **Structured log collection defaults + export integrations** (sane defaults that work in private environments)

This pack is designed so that:
- Customer platform teams can monitor and operate Redwood Private day-2 without bespoke setup.
- Redwood Support/SRE can troubleshoot efficiently using a consistent set of signals.

## What this pack is (and is not)
### In scope (v1)
- Metrics for: control plane services, API gateway, policy/routing, serving runtime pods, installer/upgrade controller, and key integration points (e.g., model registry).
- A curated set of dashboards and alerting that covers the highest-impact failure modes:
  - Availability, latency, error rate
  - Saturation (GPU/CPU/memory), queueing/backpressure
  - Restart loops / crash loops
  - Storage pressure (Prometheus TSDB PVC) and scrape health
- Log pipeline defaults for control plane and runtime logs with redaction-safe guidance.
- Packaging for **online (VPC)** and **offline/air-gapped (on-prem)** installs.

### Out of scope (v1)
- “Premium” observability: long retention defaults, multi-cluster federation, highly customized dashboards, full distributed tracing with customer-specific sampling policies.
- A Redwood-managed SIEM. Instead, we provide supported export paths to common sinks.

## Supported environments
### Deployment modes
- **Redwood Private (VPC)**: supported
- **Redwood Private (on-prem / air-gapped)**: supported (via offline bundle)

### Kubernetes and distro assumptions
The pack is validated on reference environments used by Private deployments. As a baseline, Redwood Support expects:
- A supported Kubernetes version per the current Private release notes
- A functional cluster DNS and CNI
- Ability to create PVCs (for Prometheus/Grafana as configured)

If you operate OpenShift or other distros, the pack generally works, but any required SecurityContext/PSA constraints may require customer-side adaptation.

## What’s included
### 1) Grafana dashboards (minimum supported set)
The dashboards ship as versioned JSON and are provisioned automatically when the pack is enabled.

**Dashboard set (v1):**
| Dashboard | Primary audience | What it answers |
|---|---|---|
| Private Runtime Overview | Platform / Oncall | “Are requests succeeding? What’s p95/p99 latency? How many tokens/sec? Is batching helping? Any queue buildup?” |
| Control Plane Health | Platform / Support | “Are core services healthy (gateway, auth integration, policy engine)? Any elevated 5xx or dependency failures?” |
| GPU & Node Resources | Platform / SRE | “Are GPUs saturated? Any GPU memory pressure/OOM? Node CPU/mem pressure?” |
| Prometheus / Scrape Health | Platform | “Are targets being scraped? Any stale series? Is TSDB unhealthy or out of space?” |
| Logging Pipeline Health | Platform | “Is the log collector falling behind? Dropping? Retry/backpressure?” |

Notes:
- Dashboards are designed to be **low-cardinality safe**. We avoid per-request labels and limit high-cardinality breakdowns (e.g., model/route) to aggregated or top-N views.
- Dashboards include “**What to check first**” panels for common incidents.

### 2) Prometheus alert rules + recording rules
Alerts are installed as PrometheusRule resources (when enabled). Recording rules provide stable time series for dashboards and for alert evaluation.

**Alert philosophy**
- Alerts align to Private SLO guidance and are tuned for private cluster realities (varying traffic, customer network policies, constrained resources).
- Severity is standardized:
  - **Sev1 (page)**: likely customer-visible outage or rapid SLO burn
  - **Sev2 (page or urgent ticket)**: high risk of degradation/outage if unaddressed
  - **Sev3 (ticket)**: operational issue, early warning, or configuration drift

**Minimum alert groups (v1):**
| Group | Examples | Default severity |
|---|---|---|
| Availability & Error Rate | API 5xx elevated, gateway upstream failures | Sev1/Sev2 |
| Latency & SLO Burn | p95/p99 latency burn rate, request timeouts | Sev1/Sev2 |
| GPU Saturation & OOM | GPU utilization pinned, GPU memory near limit, OOMKilled inference pods | Sev2 |
| Queueing / Backpressure | queue depth growing, batcher backlog sustained | Sev2/Sev3 |
| Restart Loops | CrashLoopBackOff on critical services | Sev2 |
| Scrape / Telemetry Health | scrape target down, rules failing to evaluate, Prometheus TSDB issues | Sev2/Sev3 |
| Storage Pressure | Prometheus PVC filling quickly, disk space low | Sev2 |

Runbook linkage:
- Each alert includes annotations with:
  - Symptom summary
  - “What to do first” steps
  - Link to the Private day-2 runbook section

### 3) Logs: collection defaults + export options
The minimum pack standardizes **structured logs** and offers supported export patterns suitable for private environments.

**Log sources (v1):**
- Control plane services (API gateway, auth/RBAC integration points, policy/routing service)
- Serving runtime pods
- Installer/upgrade controller

**Structured fields (typical):**
- `timestamp`, `level`, `service`, `component`, `namespace`, `pod`, `node`
- `request_id` (generated at gateway)
- `route` (bounded / normalized)
- `model` (bounded / normalized)
- `tenant_id_hash` (when applicable; hashed)
- `status_code`, `latency_ms`, `tokens_in`, `tokens_out` (where applicable)

**Redaction and safety**
- The minimum pack is designed to avoid secrets/PII in logs by default.
- Do not enable request/response payload logging in production unless explicitly approved by your security team.
- See: “Private observability and audit log interactions” for retention and redaction guidance.

**Supported export targets (documented patterns):**
- **Loki** (Grafana stack)
- **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
- **Splunk HEC**
- **OTLP-compatible endpoint** (where feasible; typically customer-managed collector)

## Installation and enablement
The minimum pack is shipped as a versioned artifact and wired into both:
- **`redwood-private-installer`** (recommended for most customers)
- **`redwood-helm-charts`** (for teams operating directly via Helm/GitOps)

### A) Enable via Private Installer (recommended)
1. During install configuration, set:
   - `observability.minimumPack.enabled=true`
2. Choose one of:
   - **Bring your own Prometheus/Grafana** (recommended if you already run them)
   - **Install reference Prometheus/Grafana** via the provided chart options (sizing required)
3. Choose logging mode:
   - Local collection with retention defaults
   - Export to a supported sink (Loki/ELK/Splunk/OTLP)
4. Run installer preflights:
   - Storage class and PVC provisioning
   - NetworkPolicy reachability for scrape targets
   - Resource requests/limits sanity check
5. Post-install validation:
   - Prometheus scrape health
   - Dashboards provisioned and populated
   - A small set of “canary” alerts appear (not necessarily firing)

### B) Enable via Helm (GitOps / advanced)
Enable the pack in your Private values:
- `observabilityPack.enabled: true`
- Configure Grafana provisioning (folder, datasource name)
- Configure PrometheusRule installation behavior
- Configure log collector and sink

Operational note:
- If you already have cluster-wide Prometheus/Grafana, do not install a second copy unless required. Instead, point the pack to your existing stack and ensure scrape configs include Redwood namespaces.

## Versioning strategy
### Pack versioning
- The minimum pack is versioned and released in lockstep with Redwood Private releases.
- The pack follows semantic versioning **within** the Private release stream:
  - **Patch**: dashboard fixes, threshold tuning, non-breaking alert changes
  - **Minor**: additive dashboards/alerts/metrics, new export options, recording rule additions
  - **Major**: breaking changes to metric names, dashboard schema, or required components (avoided; requires explicit migration guidance)

### Compatibility rules
- Metric names and labels are treated as an API for customers.
- We aim for backward compatibility across at least one Private minor release.
- If a breaking metric change is unavoidable:
  - We provide a documented migration
  - We include a temporary compatibility recording rule (where possible)
  - We validate upgrade and rollback paths

## Support boundary (what Redwood Support will ask for)
For tickets involving performance, errors, or instability, Redwood Support may request:
- A screenshot/export of the **Runtime Overview** and **Control Plane Health** dashboards for the incident window
- Prometheus “targets” status for Redwood scrape jobs
- Relevant structured logs filtered by `request_id`/time range
- Confirmation of:
  - Observability pack version
  - Private release version
  - Any custom NetworkPolicies or scrape restrictions

If the minimum pack is disabled or significantly modified, Support may ask you to reproduce the issue with the supported defaults to reduce time-to-resolution.

## Known limitations and operational notes
- High-cardinality breakdowns (per-tenant/per-user) are intentionally not supported in v1 to protect customer Prometheus performance.
- Trace support is limited in v1; use logs + metrics as the primary troubleshooting path.
- Air-gapped installs require that all images and dashboard/rule artifacts come from the offline bundle.

## Quick validation checklist (after install/upgrade)
1. Prometheus targets show **UP** for Redwood endpoints.
2. Grafana folder **“Redwood Private (Minimum Pack)”** exists and dashboards render.
3. The Runtime Overview shows non-zero traffic after a test request.
4. No continuous rule evaluation errors in Prometheus.
5. Log collector shows stable throughput (no sustained drop/backpressure).

## Ownership and escalation
- Product owner: **Vivek Kulkarni** (Private Deployments)
- Engineering owners:
  - Control plane observability: **Ethan Park**
  - Telemetry platform / pack plumbing: **Logan Wright**
  - SRE validation & on-call readiness: **Rafael Mendes**
- Security review: **Marcus Lin**

For day-2 procedures, see the Private Observability Day-2 Runbook.

source.title: Terraform VPC Deployment Guide (Alpha) — Redwood Private on AWS
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.body:
## Overview
This guide walks through creating the AWS networking foundation for a Redwood Private deployment using the **Redwood VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**.

**What this guide covers (alpha scope):**
- AWS VPC + subnet layout (public + private)
- Routing and NAT defaults
- Required/recommended VPC endpoints and DNS behavior
- Logging defaults (VPC Flow Logs → CloudWatch Logs or S3)
- IAM scaffolding for the **installer execution role** (permission boundary-friendly)
- Validation steps before handing off to `redwood-private-installer`

**What this guide does not cover:**
- Installing EKS / Kubernetes (handled by your platform standards; Redwood supports EKS assumptions described in the reference architecture)
- Installing Redwood Private application components (handled by `redwood-private-installer` and Helm)
- Complex enterprise network topologies (Transit Gateway, shared services VPC, multi-region, custom DNS appliances) beyond documented extension points

## Supported topology (alpha)
This guide assumes:
- Single AWS account (pilot-friendly) or a dedicated workload account in a multi-account org.
- One VPC per Redwood Private environment (dev/stage/prod).
- 2–3 Availability Zones.
- **Private subnets** for cluster nodes and internal services.
- **Public subnets** for NAT gateways (and optional load balancers depending on your ingress model).
- Optional outbound restrictions via egress security group patterns (see “Security group defaults” below).

For architecture details and trade-offs, see: `aws-private-vpc-reference-architecture-eks`.

---

## Prerequisites
### 1) Tooling
- Terraform: **>= 1.6.x** (use the repo-pinned version if provided by the module release)
- AWS Provider: repo-pinned (do not override unless directed by Infra)
- `awscli` v2 authenticated to the target account
- Optional but recommended:
  - `tflint`
  - `checkov`
  - `make` (for repo automation)

### 2) AWS account + IAM requirements
Minimum permissions depend on your org policy, but you will need:
- Ability to create/manage: VPC, subnets, route tables, IGW, NAT gateways, VPC endpoints, security groups, VPC flow logs, IAM roles/policies (if using IAM scaffold module)
- If you cannot create IAM roles directly, plan to:
  - Have a platform admin create the installer role (using the scaffold module outputs), OR
  - Use a pre-existing execution role that meets Redwood’s installer requirements

For IAM boundary posture and least-privilege guidance, see: `private-deployments-iam-boundaries-terraform`.

### 3) Inputs you must decide up front
- **AWS Region** and **AZ count** (2 or 3)
- **VPC CIDR** and subnet CIDRs (must not overlap other connected networks if using VPN/DX/TGW)
- NAT strategy:
  - Recommended for production: **one NAT Gateway per AZ**
  - Cost-optimized for pilots: **single NAT Gateway** (documented risk: AZ failure impact)
- Logging destination:
  - CloudWatch Logs (fast to set up)
  - S3 (preferred for longer retention / SIEM pipelines; may require customer-owned buckets)
- VPC endpoints strategy (recommended): enable endpoints + private DNS for core AWS services used by the installer and runtime

### 4) Redwood Private installer expectations (networking)
Before you run the installer, the environment must be able to:
- Resolve DNS reliably in private subnets (VPC resolver + correct DHCP options)
- Pull container images (typically via ECR) without requiring public internet access (recommended via VPC endpoints)
- Reach AWS APIs required for cluster operations and logging (via endpoints or controlled egress)

If you are enforcing “no public egress,” you must enable the required VPC endpoints and validate private DNS.

---

## Recommended repository layout (customer or pilot repo)
We recommend a thin wrapper repo that pins a module version and holds environment-specific `tfvars`.

Example:
- `environments/dev/main.tf`
- `environments/dev/versions.tf`
- `environments/dev/backend.tf` (optional)
- `environments/dev/dev.tfvars`

If you are using the internal quickstart template, start from: `private-vpc-terraform-quickstart-template`.

---

## Step-by-step: Deploy the VPC foundation
### Step 0 — Pick an environment name and tags
Decide on consistent naming and tags before applying.

Recommended tags:
- `Owner` (team DL or oncall alias)
- `Environment` (`dev` / `stage` / `prod`)
- `CostCenter`
- `ManagedBy=terraform`
- `RedwoodPrivate=true`

### Step 1 — Create the Terraform root module (wrapper)
In your environment directory, define module blocks for the alpha suite.

Typical module composition (alpha):
- `vpc` (VPC, subnets, routing, NAT)
- `endpoints` (Gateway + Interface endpoints)
- `logging` (Flow logs destinations)
- `security_groups` (opinionated SG patterns)
- `iam_installer_role` (optional scaffold)

Keep the wrapper small; avoid copying module internals.

### Step 2 — Configure remote state (recommended)
For team usage, configure an S3 backend with DynamoDB locking.

Operational recommendations:
- One state file per environment
- Enable S3 versioning
- Enforce encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS)
- Restrict state bucket access (least privilege)

### Step 3 — Provide minimal inputs (tfvars)
At minimum, most customers provide:
- `name_prefix`
- `region`
- `azs` (list)
- `vpc_cidr`
- `public_subnet_cidrs`
- `private_subnet_cidrs`
- `nat_strategy` (`single` or `one_per_az`)
- `enable_vpc_endpoints` (recommended: `true`)
- `enable_flow_logs` (recommended: `true`)
- `tags` (map)

If you have strict egress controls, also set:
- Endpoint private DNS behavior (enable for ECR/STS/Logs where supported)
- Explicit egress security group allowlists

**CIDR guidance (common pilot default):**
- VPC: /16
- Public subnets: /20 per AZ
- Private subnets: /20 per AZ

Your org standards may differ; the key is to avoid overlap with corporate networks if VPN/DX will be attached.

### Step 4 — Terraform init/plan
Run:
- `terraform fmt -recursive`
- `terraform init`
- `terraform validate`
- `terraform plan -var-file=dev.tfvars`

Before applying:
- Confirm the NAT strategy matches your availability requirements.
- Confirm flow log destination/retention aligns with your security policy.
- Confirm endpoints include ECR + Logs + STS if running without internet egress.

### Step 5 — Apply
Run:
- `terraform apply -var-file=dev.tfvars`

Keep the full plan/apply output for your change record (especially for regulated pilots).

### Step 6 — Capture outputs for downstream steps
Export outputs to a JSON file for handoff:
- `terraform output -json > tf-outputs.json`

You will use these values when setting up the cluster and when running the installer preflight.

---

## Post-apply validation checklist
Run these checks immediately after apply (before cluster creation or installer).

### A) VPC + subnet sanity
- VPC exists and has expected CIDR
- Public and private subnets exist in each target AZ
- Private subnets:
  - Have routes to NAT (if NAT enabled), OR
  - Have no default route if you are enforcing no-egress and relying purely on endpoints (only valid if all required endpoints are enabled)

### B) NAT + routing
- If `one_per_az`:
  - One NAT Gateway per AZ
  - Each private subnet routes to its same-AZ NAT (avoid cross-AZ data charges and AZ failure coupling)
- If `single`:
  - Acknowledge reduced resiliency; document in the environment README/runbook

### C) VPC endpoints (installer-critical)
For most private deployments, we strongly recommend endpoints for:
- ECR API and ECR DKR (image pulls)
- S3 (gateway endpoint; common dependency for artifacts)
- STS (role assumption)
- CloudWatch Logs (if logging to CW)
- EC2 (as needed by your cluster tooling)

**Private DNS:** ensure private DNS is enabled where supported, unless you have a corporate DNS design that requires explicit split-horizon control.

Validation commands (examples):
- Confirm endpoints exist in the VPC
- From a test instance in a private subnet, verify DNS resolution:
  - `nslookup api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
  - `nslookup <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

If ECR endpoints or private DNS are missing/misconfigured, you will typically see installer failures pulling images.

### D) DNS reliability
Ensure:
- `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames` are enabled on the VPC
- DHCP options set is appropriate for your environment (if customized)

Known failure mode in pilots: intermittent DNS resolution in private subnets due to conflicting resolver setups or custom DHCP options. If you see intermittent resolution, stop and fix DNS before proceeding.

### E) Logging defaults
Confirm flow logs are delivering to the configured destination:
- CloudWatch: log group exists, has retention set, and receives records
- S3: bucket policy allows delivery, objects are landing in expected prefix

For security expectations and SIEM patterns, see: `private-vpc-logging-defaults-and-siem-hooks`.

### F) Security group defaults
Confirm the module-created security groups match your intended posture:
- No broad inbound rules except those explicitly required (e.g., internal cluster communication)
- Egress rules align with your egress strategy:
  - Endpoint-only + restricted egress, OR
  - NAT-based with controlled outbound

---

## Handoff to cluster provisioning (EKS) and Redwood Private installer
### 1) Cluster provisioning expectations
This Terraform guide does not create the cluster. You (or your platform team) will typically:
- Create an EKS cluster using private subnets
- Ensure node groups launch in private subnets
- Configure cluster logging per your standards

Inputs you will commonly need from this module suite:
- `vpc_id`
- `private_subnet_ids`
- `public_subnet_ids` (if using public LBs/NAT)
- Security group IDs (as applicable)
- Endpoint details (for validation and troubleshooting)

### 2) Run installer preflight checks
After the cluster exists (and before a full install), run the installer preflight.

Recommended workflow:
1. Export Terraform outputs:
   - `terraform output -json > tf-outputs.json`
2. Run preflight (exact command may vary by installer version):
   - `redwood-private-installer preflight --tf-outputs tf-outputs.json`

Preflight will validate common network prerequisites such as:
- Required endpoints presence (notably ECR)
- DNS expectations
- Obvious misconfigurations that frequently break installs

### 3) Provide required artifacts to Redwood (pilot support)
For alpha pilots/design partners, provide:
- Module versions pinned (git tag or registry version)
- `tf-outputs.json` (sanitize if required by your policy)
- Region + AZs
- Any custom DNS / proxy / egress constraints

---

## Troubleshooting (common alpha issues)
### Issue: Installer fails pulling images (ECR)
Symptoms:
- Pods stuck in `ImagePullBackOff`
- Installer logs reference ECR endpoints or timeouts

Likely causes:
- Missing ECR interface endpoints
- Private DNS disabled for ECR endpoints
- Route/NACL/SG blocking endpoint traffic

Fix:
- Enable ECR API + ECR DKR endpoints
- Enable private DNS (unless you have a controlled split-horizon alternative)
- Re-run preflight

### Issue: Intermittent DNS resolution in private subnets
Symptoms:
- Flaky service discovery
- Random AWS API timeouts from private subnets

Likely causes:
- Incorrect VPC DNS settings
- Custom DHCP options pointing to resolvers that are not reachable from private subnets
- Resolver rules that conflict with AWS private hosted zones / endpoint private DNS

Fix:
- Validate VPC DNS flags and DHCP options
- Test from an instance in the private subnet repeatedly; treat intermittent DNS as a blocking infrastructure issue

### Issue: Flow logs not delivered
Symptoms:
- No records in CloudWatch log group or S3 prefix

Likely causes:
- IAM permissions for delivery not present
- Incorrect log destination ARN
- KMS policy blocks encryption (if SSE-KMS)

Fix:
- Validate log destination policies and (if applicable) KMS key policy

---

## Change management and rollback guidance (alpha)
Some changes are high-risk after downstream resources exist (EKS, endpoints in use, production traffic). Treat the following as potentially breaking:
- Changing subnet CIDRs or replacing subnets
- Changing NAT strategy (single ↔ one-per-AZ) without a plan
- Removing endpoints or toggling private DNS
- Tightening security groups without validating cluster/installer traffic

Before upgrading module versions or making structural changes, follow: `terraform-module-upgrade-and-rollback-procedure`.

---

## Getting help / escalation (alpha support model)
For design partner pilots:
- First line: Solutions Engineering / Pro Services channel and your assigned Redwood pilot partner
- Engineering escalation: `#eng-private-deployments` with:
  - module version
  - region
  - `terraform plan` diff (redact as needed)
  - installer preflight output

Security/logging questions:
- `#eng-security` (include intended log destinations and retention requirements)

---

## Appendix: Quick validation from a private subnet (optional)
If you have a test instance (or SSM session) in a private subnet, validate:
- DNS:
  - `nslookup sts.<region>.amazonaws.com`
  - `nslookup api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
- Connectivity (endpoint path vs internet):
  - `curl -I https://sts.<region>.amazonaws.com` (expects an HTTP response)
- Route awareness:
  - Confirm traffic to AWS services is not exiting via IGW when you intend endpoint-only

Record results in your environment runbook; these checks reduce time-to-diagnosis during installer runs.

title: Terraform VPC Deployment Guide (Alpha) — Redwood Private on AWS
preview:
## Overview
This guide walks through creating the AWS networking foundation for a Redwood Private deployment using the **Redwood VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**.

**What this guide covers (alpha scope):**
- AWS VPC + subnet layout (public + private)
- Routing and NAT defaults
- Required/recommended VPC endpoints and DNS behavior
- Logging defaults (VPC Flow Logs → CloudWatch Logs or S3)
- IAM scaffolding for the **installer execution role** (permission boundary-friendly)
- Validation steps before handing off to `redwood-private-installer`

**What this guide does not cover:**
- Installing EKS / Kubernetes (handled by your platform standards; Redwood supports EKS assumptions described in the reference architecture)
- Installing Redwood Private application components (handled by `redwood-private-installer` and Helm)
- Complex enterprise network topologies (Transit Gateway, shared services VPC, multi-region, custom DNS appliances) beyond documented extension points

## Supported topology (alpha)
This guide assumes:
- Single AWS account (pilot-friendly) or a dedicated workload account in a multi-account org.
- One VPC per Redwood Private environment (dev/stage/prod).
- 2–3 Availability Zones.
- **Private subnets** for cluster nodes and internal services.
- **Public subnets** for NAT gateways (and optional load balancers depending on your ingress model).
- Optional outbound restrictions via egress security group patterns (see “Security group defaults” below).

For architecture details and trade-offs, see: `aws-private-vpc-reference-architecture-eks`.

---

## Prerequisites
### 1) Tooling
- Terraform: **>= 1.6.x** (use the repo-pinned version if provided by the module release)
- AWS Provider: repo-pinned (do not override unless directed by Infra)
- `awscli` v2 authenticated to the target account
- Optional but recommended:
  - `tflint`
  - `checkov`
  - `make` (for repo automation)

### 2) AWS account + IAM requirements
Minimum permissions depend on your org policy, but you will need:
- Ability to create/manage: VPC, subnets, route tables, IGW, NAT gateways, VPC endpoints, security groups, VPC flow logs, IAM roles/policies (if using IAM scaffold module)
- If you cannot create IAM roles directly, plan to:
  - Have a platform admin create the installer role (using the scaffold module outputs), OR
  - Use a pre-existing execution role that meets Redwood’s installer requirements

For IAM boundary posture and least-privilege guidance, see: `private-deployments-iam-boundaries-terraform`.

### 3) Inputs you must decide up front
- **AWS Region** and **AZ count** (2 or 3)
- **VPC CIDR** and subnet CIDRs (must not overlap other connected networks if using VPN/DX/TGW)
- NAT strategy:
  - Recommended for production: **one NAT Gateway per AZ**
  - Cost-optimized for pilots: **single NAT Gateway** (documented risk: AZ failure impact)
- Logging destination:
  - CloudWatch Logs (fast to set up)
  - S3 (preferred for longer retention / SIEM pipelines; may require customer-owned buckets)
- VPC endpoints strategy (recommended): enable endpoints + private DNS for core AWS services used by the installer and runtime

### 4) Redwood Private installer expectations (networking)
Before you run the installer, the environment must be able to:
- Resolve DNS reliably in private subnets (VPC resolver + correct DHCP options)
- Pull container images (typically via ECR) without requiring public internet access (recommended via VPC endpoints)
- Reach AWS APIs required for cluster operations and logging (via endpoints or controlled egress)

If you are enforcing “no public egress,” you must enable the required VPC endpoints and validate private DNS.

---

## Recommended repository layout (customer or pilot repo)
We recommend a thin wrapper repo that pins a module version and holds environment-specific `tfvars`.

Example:
- `environments/dev/main.tf`
- `environments/dev/versions.tf`
- `environments/dev/backend.tf` (optional)
- `environments/dev/dev.tfvars`

If you are using the internal quickstart template, start from: `private-vpc-terraform-quickstart-template`.

---

## Step-by-step: Deploy the VPC foundation
### Step 0 — Pick an environment name and tags
Decide on consistent naming and tags before applying.

Recommended tags:
- `Owner` (team DL or oncall alias)
- `Environment` (`dev` / `stage` / `prod`)
- `CostCenter`
- `ManagedBy=terraform`
- `RedwoodPrivate=true`

### Step 1 — Create the Terraform root module (wrapper)
In your environment directory, define module blocks for the alpha suite.

Typical module composition (alpha):
- `vpc` (VPC, subnets, routing, NAT)
- `endpoints` (Gateway + Interface endpoints)
- `logging` (Flow logs destinations)
- `security_groups` (opinionated SG patterns)
- `iam_installer_role` (optional scaffold)

Keep the wrapper small; avoid copying module internals.

### Step 2 — Configure remote state (recommended)
For team usage, configure an S3 backend with DynamoDB locking.

Operational recommendations:
- One state file per environment
- Enable S3 versioning
- Enforce encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS)
- Restrict state bucket access (least privilege)

### Step 3 — Provide minimal inputs (tfvars)
At minimum, most customers provide:
- `name_prefix`
- `region`
- `azs` (list)
- `vpc_cidr`
- `public_subnet_cidrs`
- `private_subnet_cidrs`
- `nat_strategy` (`single` or `one_per_az`)
- `enable_vpc_endpoints` (recommended: `true`)
- `enable_flow_logs` (recommended: `true`)
- `tags` (map)

If you have strict egress controls, also set:
- Endpoint private DNS behavior (enable for ECR/STS/Logs where supported)
- Explicit egress security group allowlists

**CIDR guidance (common pilot default):**
- VPC: /16
- Public subnets: /20 per AZ
- Private subnets: /20 per AZ

Your org standards may differ; the key is to avoid overlap with corporate networks if VPN/DX will be attached.

### Step 4 — Terraform init/plan
Run:
- `terraform fmt -recursive`
- `terraform init`
- `terraform validate`
- `terraform plan -var-file=dev.tfvars`

Before applying:
- Confirm the NAT strategy matches your availability requirements.
- Confirm flow log destination/retention aligns with your security policy.
- Confirm endpoints include ECR + Logs + STS if running without internet egress.

### Step 5 — Apply
Run:
- `terraform apply -var-file=dev.tfvars`

Keep the full plan/apply output for your change record (especially for regulated pilots).

### Step 6 — Capture outputs for downstream steps
Export outputs to a JSON file for handoff:
- `terraform output -json > tf-outputs.json`

You will use these values when setting up the cluster and when running the installer preflight.

---

## Post-apply validation checklist
Run these checks immediately after apply (before cluster creation or installer).

### A) VPC + subnet sanity
- VPC exists and has expected CIDR
- Public and private subnets exist in each target AZ
- Private subnets:
  - Have routes to NAT (if NAT enabled), OR
  - Have no default route if you are enforcing no-egress and relying purely on endpoints (only valid if all required endpoints are enabled)

### B) NAT + routing
- If `one_per_az`:
  - One NAT Gateway per AZ
  - Each private subnet routes to its same-AZ NAT (avoid cross-AZ data charges and AZ failure coupling)
- If `single`:
  - Acknowledge reduced resiliency; document in the environment README/runbook

### C) VPC endpoints (installer-critical)
For most private deployments, we strongly recommend endpoints for:
- ECR API and ECR DKR (image pulls)
- S3 (gateway endpoint; common dependency for artifacts)
- STS (role assumption)
- CloudWatch Logs (if logging to CW)
- EC2 (as needed by your cluster tooling)

**Private DNS:** ensure private DNS is enabled where supported, unless you have a corporate DNS design that requires explicit split-horizon control.

Validation commands (examples):
- Confirm endpoints exist in the VPC
- From a test instance in a private subnet, verify DNS resolution:
  - `nslookup api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
  - `nslookup <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

If ECR endpoints or private DNS are missing/misconfigured, you will typically see installer failures pulling images.

### D) DNS reliability
Ensure:
- `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames` are enabled on the VPC
- DHCP options set is appropriate for your environment (if customized)

Known failure mode in pilots: intermittent DNS resolution in private subnets due to conflicting resolver setups or custom DHCP options. If you see intermittent resolution, stop and fix DNS before proceeding.

### E) Logging defaults
Confirm flow logs are delivering to the configured destination:
- CloudWatch: log group exists, has retention set, and receives records
- S3: bucket policy allows delivery, objects are landing in expected prefix

For security expectations and SIEM patterns, see: `private-vpc-logging-defaults-and-siem-hooks`.

### F) Security group defaults
Confirm the module-created security groups match your intended posture:
- No broad inbound rules except those explicitly required (e.g., internal cluster communication)
- Egress rules align with your egress strategy:
  - Endpoint-only + restricted egress, OR
  - NAT-based with controlled outbound

---

## Handoff to cluster provisioning (EKS) and Redwood Private installer
### 1) Cluster provisioning expectations
This Terraform guide does not create the cluster. You (or your platform team) will typically:
- Create an EKS cluster using private subnets
- Ensure node groups launch in private subnets
- Configure cluster logging per your standards

Inputs you will commonly need from this module suite:
- `vpc_id`
- `private_subnet_ids`
- `public_subnet_ids` (if using public LBs/NAT)
- Security group IDs (as applicable)
- Endpoint details (for validation and troubleshooting)

### 2) Run installer preflight checks
After the cluster exists (and before a full install), run the installer preflight.

Recommended workflow:
1. Export Terraform outputs:
   - `terraform output -json > tf-outputs.json`
2. Run preflight (exact command may vary by installer version):
   - `redwood-private-installer preflight --tf-outputs tf-outputs.json`

Preflight will validate common network prerequisites such as:
- Required endpoints presence (notably ECR)
- DNS expectations
- Obvious misconfigurations that frequently break installs

### 3) Provide required artifacts to Redwood (pilot support)
For alpha pilots/design partners, provide:
- Module versions pinned (git tag or registry version)
- `tf-outputs.json` (sanitize if required by your policy)
- Region + AZs
- Any custom DNS / proxy / egress constraints

---

## Troubleshooting (common alpha issues)
### Issue: Installer fails pulling images (ECR)
Symptoms:
- Pods stuck in `ImagePullBackOff`
- Installer logs reference ECR endpoints or timeouts

Likely causes:
- Missing ECR interface endpoints
- Private DNS disabled for ECR endpoints
- Route/NACL/SG blocking endpoint traffic

Fix:
- Enable ECR API + ECR DKR endpoints
- Enable private DNS (unless you have a controlled split-horizon alternative)
- Re-run preflight

### Issue: Intermittent DNS resolution in private subnets
Symptoms:

- Random AWS API timeouts from private subnets

Likely causes:

- Custom DHCP options pointing to resolvers that are not reachable from private subnets
- Resolver rules that conflict with AWS private hosted zones / endpoint private DNS

Fix:
- Validate VPC DNS flags and DHCP options
- Test from an instance in the private subnet repeatedly; treat intermittent DNS as a blocking infrastructure issue

### Issue: Flow logs not delivered
Symptoms:
- No records in CloudWatch log group or S3 prefix

Likely causes:
- IAM permissions for delivery not present
- Incorrect log destination ARN
- KMS policy blocks encryption (if SSE-KMS)

Fix:
- Validate log destination policies and (if applicable) KMS key policy

---

## Change management and rollback guidance (alpha)
Some changes are high-risk after downstream resources exist (EKS, endpoints in use, production traffic). Treat the following as potentially breaking:
- Changing subnet CIDRs or replacing subnets
- Changing NAT strategy (single ↔ one-per-AZ) without a plan
- Removing endpoints or toggling private DNS
- Tightening security groups without validating cluster/installer traffic

Before upgrading module versions or making structural changes, follow: `terraform-module-upgrade-and-rollback-procedure`.

---

## Getting help / escalation (alpha support model)
For design partner pilots:
- First line: Solutions Engineering / Pro Services channel and your assigned Redwood pilot partner
- Engineering escalation: `#eng-private-deployments` with:
  - module version
  - region
  - `terraform plan` diff (redact as needed)
  - installer preflight output

Security/logging questions:
- `#eng-security` (include intended log destinations and retention requirements)

---

## Appendix: Quick validation from a private subnet (optional)
If you have a test instance (or SSM session) in a private subnet, validate:
- DNS:
  - `nslookup sts.<region>.amazonaws.com`
  - `nslookup api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
- Connectivity (endpoint path vs internet):
  - `curl -I https://sts.<region>.amazonaws.com` (expects an HTTP response)
